With this dataset, we observe that humans achieve substantially higher accuracy compared to LLMs, which demonstrates that humans are more effective in trial-and-error than LLMs. We believe that the TEC platform and dataset provide a valuable foundation for understanding human trial-and-error behavior and for developing more capable AI systems. Platform and dataset are publicly available.

</details>


### [91] [SQLStructEval: Structural Evaluation of LLM Text-to-SQL Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06736)
*Yixi Zhou,Fan Zhang,Zhiqiao Guo,Yu Chen,Haipeng Zhang,Preslav Nakov,Zhuohan Xie*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates the structural reliability of LLM-generated SQL queries and introduces SQLStructEval, a framework to analyze their structures using canonical abstract syntax trees (AST).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Though LLMs perform well on Text-to-SQL benchmarks, concerns remain about whether their generated SQL structures are consistent and reliable, an issue that this research aims to explore.

Method: The authors propose SQLStructEval for analyzing SQL program structure via AST and conduct experiments on the Spider benchmark to study structural diversity and consistencies under varying conditions.

Result: Experiments reveal that LLMs generate structurally diverse SQL queries, even for identical inputs, with structural reliability impacted by minor input changes like schema presentation or paraphrasing.

Conclusion: Improving the structural generation process, such as through a compile-style pipeline, can enhance both execution accuracy and structural consistency, highlighting the importance of structural evaluation in program generation systems.

Abstract: Despite strong performance on Text-to-SQL benchmarks, it remains unclear whether LLM-generated SQL programs are structurally reliable. In this work, we investigate the structural behavior of LLM-generated SQL queries and introduce SQLStructEval, a framework for analyzing program structures through canonical abstract syntax tree (AST) representations. Our experiments on the Spider benchmark show that modern LLMs often produce structurally diverse queries for the same input, even when execution results are correct, and that such variance is frequently triggered by surface-level input changes such as paraphrases or schema presentation. We further show that generating queries in a structured space via a compile-style pipeline can improve both execution accuracy and structural consistency. These findings suggest that structural reliability is a critical yet overlooked dimension for evaluating LLM-based program generation systems. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/StructEval-2435.

</details>


### [92] [Luwen Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06737)
*Yiquan Wu,Yuhang Liu,Yifei Liu,Ang Li,Siying Zhou,Kun Kuang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Luwen, a specialized Chinese legal language model, was developed using Baichuan and outperformed existing baselines across various legal tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in applying large language models to the legal domain, such as specialized terminology and complex reasoning.

Method: The model was developed via continual pre-training on legal corpus, supervised fine-tuning with curated data, and retrieval-augmented generation.

Result: Luwen outperformed strong baselines on five legal tasks, demonstrating its effectiveness.

Conclusion: The approach successfully adapted a general-purpose language model to the Chinese legal domain, setting a performance benchmark for future research.

Abstract: Large language models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across a wide range of natural language processing tasks, yet their application in the legal domain remains challenging due to the specialized terminology, complex reasoning requirements, and rapidly evolving legal knowledge involved. In this paper, we present Luwen, an open-source Chinese legal language model built upon the Baichuan foundation model through three key techniques: continual pre-training on a large-scale legal corpus, supervised fine-tuning with carefully curated legal instruction data, and retrieval-augmented generation integrated with a comprehensive legal knowledge base. We evaluate Luwen on five representative legal tasks spanning both prediction and generation settings, including legal judgment prediction, judicial examination, legal text summarization, law article question answering, and judicial decision reasoning. Experimental results show that Luwen outperforms several strong baselines, demonstrating the effectiveness of our approach in adapting general-purpose language models to the legal domain.

</details>


### [93] [StructKV: Preserving the Structural Skeleton for Scalable Long-Context Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06746)
*Zhirui Chen,Peiyang Liu,Ling Shao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: StructKV is a framework designed to compress Key-Value (KV) caches in Large Language Models (LLMs) for efficient long-context inference, addressing scalability bottlenecks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle the memory and bandwidth bottlenecks associated with the linear growth of KV caches as LLMs support increasingly longer context windows.

Method: StructKV introduces three innovations: (1) Global In-Degree Centrality to identify global information hubs, (2) Dynamic Pivot Detection for choosing the optimal layer for compression, and (3) Structural Propagation and Decoupling to separate computational and memory storage budgets.

Result: Experiments on LongBench and RULER benchmarks show that StructKV successfully preserves long-range dependencies and retrieval robustness.

Conclusion: By implementing structure-aware KV cache compression, StructKV addresses the inefficiencies of long-context inference in LLMs, enabling better scalability and memory management.

Abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) scale to support context windows exceeding one million tokens, the linear growth of Key-Value (KV) cache imposes severe memory capacity and bandwidth bottlenecks, constraining the efficiency of long-context inference. Existing compression approaches typically prioritize tokens based on local saliency metrics to decouple prefill computation from decoding memory. However, these methods often rely on local saliency snapshots at a specific layer, thereby systematically discarding tokens that act as global information hubs across the network depth but appear temporarily dormant at the specific layer selected for pruning. To address this limitation, we propose StructKV, a structure-aware KV cache compression framework that introduces three core innovations: First, Global In-Degree Centrality aggregates attention patterns across the network depth to identify global information hubs. Second, Dynamic Pivot Detection utilizes information-theoretic metrics to adaptively locate the optimal layer for compression. Finally, Structural Propagation and Decoupling separates the computational budget from the memory storage budget. Experimental results on the LongBench and RULER benchmarks demonstrate that StructKV effectively preserves long-range dependencies and retrieval robustness.

</details>


### [94] [Select-then-Solve: Paradigm Routing as Inference-Time Optimization for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06753)
*Heng Zhou,Zelin Tan,Zhemeng Zhang,Yutao Fan,Yibing Lin,Li Kang,Xiufeng Song,Rui Li,Songtao Huang,Ao Yu,Yuchen Fan,Yanxu Chen,Kaixin Xu,Xiaohong Liu,Yiran Qin,Philip Torr,Chen Zhang,Zhenfei Yin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This study investigates whether task improvement using LLMs stems from the model itself or the reasoning paradigm applied. It compares six paradigms across models and tasks, concluding that no paradigm universally dominates and a learned router optimally selects the best paradigm per task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to understand the impact of reasoning paradigms on task performance and to propose a system for paradigm selection that optimizes the outcomes based on complementarity.

Method: Researchers conducted experiments across six reasoning paradigms, four LLMs, and ten benchmarks (18,000 runs total). They also introduced a lightweight embedding-based router to select paradigms per task dynamically.

Result: Findings show reasoning paradigms influence task performance variably, with paradigm selection via the router improving average accuracy to 53.1%, outperforming fixed paradigms by 2.8pp and recovering 37% of the oracle gap.

Conclusion: Paradigm selection should be task-specific and performed by a learned router, rather than sticking to a fixed reasoning structure for optimal performance across tasks.

Abstract: When an LLM-based agent improves on a task, is the gain from the model itself or from the reasoning paradigm wrapped around it? We study this question by comparing six inference-time paradigms, namely Direct, CoT, ReAct, Plan-Execute, Reflection, and ReCode, across four frontier LLMs and ten benchmarks, yielding roughly 18,000 runs. We find that reasoning structure helps dramatically on some tasks but hurts on others: ReAct improves over Direct by 44pp on GAIA, while CoT degrades performance by 15pp on HumanEval. No single paradigm dominates, and oracle per-task selection beats the best fixed paradigm by 17.1pp on average. Motivated by this complementarity, we propose a select-then-solve approach: before answering each task, a lightweight embedding-based router selects the most suitable paradigm. Across four models, the router improves average accuracy from 47.6% to 53.1%, outperforming the best fixed paradigm at 50.3% by 2.8pp and recovering up to 37% of the oracle gap. In contrast, zero-shot self-routing only works for GPT-5 at 67.1% and fails for weaker models, all trailing the learned router. Our results argue that reasoning paradigm selection should be a per-task decision made by a learned router, not a fixed architectural choice.

</details>


### [95] [How Long Reasoning Chains Influence LLMs' Judgment of Answer Factuality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06756)
*Minzhu Tu,Shiyu Ni,Keping Bi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The study explores how reasoning chains influence judgment accuracy within LLM-based evaluation, finding both strengths and weaknesses among weak and strong judges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess whether exposing reasoning chains to LLM judges improves their accuracy in evaluating answer correctness.

Method: Systematic investigation involving experiments with LLM judges on factual QA and mathematical reasoning benchmarks, analyzing reactions to reasoning fluency and factuality.

Result: Weak judges tend to accept incorrect answers with fluent reasoning, while strong judges leverage reasoning partially but can still be misled. Fluency and factuality were identified as critical evaluation signals.

Conclusion: Robust LLM judges are needed to discern genuine reasoning quality from superficial fluency in modern reasoning models.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) has been widely adopted as a scalable surrogate for human evaluation, yet such judges remain imperfect and susceptible to surface-level biases. One possible reason is that these judges lack sufficient information in assessing answer correctness. With the rise of reasoning-capable models, exposing a generator's reasoning content to the judge provides richer information and is a natural candidate for improving judgment accuracy. However, its actual impact on judge behavior remains understudied. In this paper, we systematically investigate how access to reasoning chains affects LLM-based judgment across factual question answering (QA) and mathematical reasoning benchmarks. We find that weak judges are easily swayed by reasoning presence, frequently accepting incorrect answers accompanied by fluent reasoning, while strong judges can partially leverage reasoning as informative evidence. Nevertheless, even strong judges are misled by seemingly high-quality reasoning chains. Controlled experiments further reveal that both fluency and factuality of reasoning chains are critical signals driving judge decisions. These findings highlight the need for more robust LLM judges that can distinguish genuine reasoning quality from superficial fluency when evaluating modern reasoning models.

</details>


### [96] [Multilingual Cognitive Impairment Detection in the Era of Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06758)
*Damar Hoogland,Boshko Koloski,Jaya Caporusso,Tine Kolenik,Ana Zwitter Vitez,Senja Pollak,Christina Manouilidou,Matthew Purver*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper compares zero-shot large language models with supervised tabular classifiers for classifying cognitive impairment across languages using speech transcripts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve cognitive impairment detection across diverse languages using speech transcripts and assess the potential of zero-shot and supervised approaches.

Method: Zero-shot large language models were tested as classifiers under transcript-only, linguistic-features-only, and combined inputs, alongside supervised tabular models trained with embedding and feature fusion.

Result: Supervised tabular models generally outperformed zero-shot LLMs, especially when using engineered linguistic features and combined modalities.

Conclusion: For small-data CI detection, structured linguistic features and fusion-based classifiers remain effective, with limited supervision yielding language-dependent benefits.

Abstract: We evaluate cognitive impairment (CI) classification from transcripts of speech in English, Slovene, and Korean. We compare zero-shot large language models (LLMs) used as direct classifiers under three input settings -- transcript-only, linguistic-features-only, and combined -- with supervised tabular approaches trained under a leave-one-out protocol. The tabular models operate on engineered linguistic features, transcript embeddings, and early or late fusion of both modalities. Across languages, zero-shot LLMs provide competitive no-training baselines, but supervised tabular models generally perform better, particularly when engineered linguistic features are included and combined with embeddings. Few-shot experiments focusing on embeddings indicate that the value of limited supervision is language-dependent, with some languages benefiting substantially from additional labelled examples while others remain constrained without richer feature representations. Overall, the results suggest that, in small-data CI detection, structured linguistic signals and simple fusion-based classifiers remain strong and reliable signals.

</details>


### [97] [TeamLLM: A Human-Like Team-Oriented Collaboration Framework for Multi-Step Contextualized Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06765)
*Xiangyu Wang,Jin Wu,Haoran Shi,Wei Xia,Jiarui Yu,Chanjin Zheng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes TeamLLM, a multi-LLM framework inspired by human team role division, for improving multi-step contextualized task performance, along with a new benchmark for evaluation called CGPST.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multi-LLM frameworks fail to emulate human-like role division, leading to a single perspective and weaker performance on multi-step contextualized tasks.

Method: TeamLLM introduces a human-like multi-LLM collaboration framework with four distinct team roles and a three-phase collaboration approach. It also proposes the CGPST benchmark to evaluate contextualized tasks.

Result: TeamLLM significantly improves performance on the proposed CGPST benchmark, demonstrated through testing ten popular LLMs across various levels of evaluation.

Conclusion: TeamLLM's human-like role division framework outperforms existing multi-LLM models on contextualized tasks, offering a new benchmark for comprehensive evaluation of LLMs.

Abstract: Recently, multi-Large Language Model (LLM) frameworks have been proposed to solve contextualized tasks. However, these frameworks do not explicitly emulate human team role division, which may lead to a single perspective, thereby weakening performance on multi-step contextualized tasks. To address this issue, we propose TeamLLM, a human-like Team-Oriented Multi-LLM Collaboration Framework. TeamLLM adopts four team roles with distinct division and employs a three-phase multi-LLM collaboration for multi-step contextualized tasks. To evaluate the effectiveness of TeamLLM on multi-step contextualized tasks, we propose Contextually-Grounded and Procedurally-Structured tasks (CGPST) and construct the CGPST benchmark. This benchmark has four core features: contextual grounding, procedural structure, process-oriented evaluation and multi-dimensional assessment. We evaluate ten popular LLMs on CGPST at overall-level, step-level, and dimension-level. Results show that TeamLLM substantially improves performance on CGPST. We release the benchmark with scenarios, full-process responses and human scores from ten LLMs. The code and data are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/TeamLLM-anonymous-C50E/.

</details>


### [98] [Multi-Faceted Self-Consistent Preference Alignment for Query Rewriting in Conversational Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06771)
*Zhiyu Cao,Peifeng Li,Qiaoming Zhu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: MSPA-CQR enhances conversational query rewriting by considering feedback from rewriting, retrieval, and response generation, showing improved effectiveness across scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Early methods ignored feedback loops from query rewriting to retrieval and response generation, limiting effectiveness in conversational search.

Method: Construct self-consistent preference alignment data across three dimensions, then apply prefix-guided multi-faceted direct preference optimization to learn preference information.

Result: MSPA-CQR improves the diversity and effectiveness of rewritten queries for better performance in conversational search.

Conclusion: Incorporating feedback from multiple dimensions yields effective and adaptable conversational query rewrites in various scenarios.

Abstract: Conversational Query Rewriting (CQR) aims to rewrite ambiguous queries to achieve more efficient conversational search. Early studies have predominantly focused on the rewriting in isolation, ignoring the feedback from query rewrite, passage retrieval and response generation in the rewriting process. To address this issue, we propose Multi-Faceted Self-Consistent Preference Aligned CQR (MSPA-CQR). Specifically, we first construct self-consistent preference alignment data from three dimensions (rewriting, retrieval, and response) to generate more diverse rewritten queries. Then we propose prefix guided multi-faceted direct preference optimization to learn preference information from three different dimensions. The experimental results show that our MSPA-CQR is effective in both in- and out-of-distribution scenarios.

</details>


### [99] [Discourse Coherence and Response-Guided Context Rewriting for Multi-Party Dialogue Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06784)
*Zhiyu Cao,Peifeng Li,Qiaoming Zhu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes DRCR, a method that uses context rewriting and response-guided feedback to improve multi-party dialogue generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of colloquial expressions and incomplete utterances that weaken dialogue structures in multi-party conversations, impeding accurate generation.

Method: The method introduces a framework (DRCR) that combines discourse coherence and response quality signals for context rewriting and employs a dynamic iterative training process for mutually enhancing models.

Result: Experimental results conducted on four multi-party dialogue datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the DRCR framework.

Conclusion: DRCR enhances the generation quality of multi-party dialogues by addressing structure fidelity issues through context rewriting and iterative interaction methods.

Abstract: Previous research on multi-party dialogue generation has predominantly leveraged structural information inherent in dialogues to directly inform the generation process. However, the prevalence of colloquial expressions and incomplete utterances in dialogues often impedes comprehension and weakens the fidelity of dialogue structure representations, which is particularly pronounced in multi-party dialogues. In this work, we propose a novel framework DRCR (Discourse coherence and Response-guided Context Rewriting) to improve multi-party dialogue generation through dialogue context rewriting. Specifically, DRCR employs two complementary feedback signals, discourse coherence and response quality, to construct preference data for both context rewriting and response generation. Moreover, we propose a dynamic self-evolution learning method that allows the rewriter and responder to continuously enhance their capabilities through mutual interaction in an iterative training loop. Comprehensive experiments conducted on four multi-party dialogue datasets substantiate the effectiveness of DRCR.

</details>


### [100] [When Is Thinking Enough? Early Exit via Sufficiency Assessment for Efficient Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06787)
*Yang Xiang,Yixin Ji,Ruotao Xu,Dan Qiao,Zheming Yang,Juntao Li,Min Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Dynamic Thought Sufficiency in Reasoning (DTSR), a framework for efficient reasoning in large reasoning models by dynamically determining optimal early exit points to reduce overthinking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address computational redundancy and inefficiency caused by overthinking in large reasoning models during complex tasks.

Method: DTSR uses a two-stage framework inspired by human metacognition: Reflection Signal Monitoring for potential early exit cues, and Thought Sufficiency Check to assess reasoning adequacy and derive answers.

Result: Tests using Qwen3 models showed DTSR successfully reduced reasoning length by 28.9%-34.9% with minimal performance loss, significantly improving efficiency.

Conclusion: DTSR mitigates overthinking in LRMs and advances efficient reasoning, alongside providing insights into overconfidence and self-evaluation paradigms.

Abstract: Large reasoning models (LRMs) have achieved remarkable performance in complex reasoning tasks, driven by their powerful inference-time scaling capability. However, LRMs often suffer from overthinking, which results in substantial computational redundancy and significantly reduces efficiency. Early-exit methods aim to mitigate this issue by terminating reasoning once sufficient evidence has been generated, yet existing approaches mostly rely on handcrafted or empirical indicators that are unreliable and impractical. In this work, we introduce Dynamic Thought Sufficiency in Reasoning (DTSR), a novel framework for efficient reasoning that enables the model to dynamically assess the sufficiency of its chain-of-thought (CoT) and determine the optimal point for early exit. Inspired by human metacognition, DTSR operates in two stages: (1) Reflection Signal Monitoring, which identifies reflection signals as potential cues for early exit, and (2) Thought Sufficiency Check, which evaluates whether the current CoT is sufficient to derive the final answer. Experimental results on the Qwen3 models show that DTSR reduces reasoning length by 28.9%-34.9% with minimal performance loss, effectively mitigating overthinking. We further discuss overconfidence in LRMs and self-evaluation paradigms, providing valuable insights for early-exit reasoning.

</details>


### [101] [GCoT-Decoding: Unlocking Deep Reasoning Paths for Universal Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06794)
*Guanran Luo,Wentao Qiu,Zhongquan Jian,Meihong Wang,Qingqiang Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The authors propose GCoT-decoding, a general decoding strategy for enhancing reasoning abilities of large language models across both fixed and free QA tasks through a novel multi-step method.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manually designed prompts for chain-of-thought reasoning in large language models are restrictive, and current CoT-decoding methods are limited to fixed answer sets. There is a need for a more generalizable approach.

Method: GCoT-decoding uses a two-stage branching method with Fibonacci sampling and heuristic error backtracking to generate reasoning paths. It computes path confidence by separating reasoning and answer spans, and uses semantic aggregation for consensus instead of majority voting.

Result: The method achieves significant improvements in free QA tasks while maintaining strong performance in fixed QA tasks, validated through experiments on six diverse datasets.

Conclusion: GCoT-decoding broadens the applicability of chain-of-thought reasoning to both fixed and free QA tasks, offering a general and robust approach.

Abstract: Chain-of-Thought reasoning can enhance large language models, but it requires manually designed prompts to guide the model. Recently proposed CoT-decoding enables the model to generate CoT-style reasoning paths without prompts, but it is only applicable to problems with fixed answer sets. To address this limitation, we propose a general decoding strategy GCoT-decoding that extends applicability to a broader range of question-answering tasks. GCoT-decoding employs a two-stage branching method combining Fibonacci sampling and heuristic error backtracking to generate candidate decoding paths. It then splits each path into a reasoning span and an answer span to accurately compute path confidence, and finally aggregates semantically similar paths to identify a consensus answer, replacing traditional majority voting. We conduct extensive experiments on six datasets covering both fixed and free QA tasks. Our method not only maintains strong performance on fixed QA but also achieves significant improvements on free QA, demonstrating its generality.

</details>


### [102] [Beyond Accuracy: Diagnosing Algebraic Reasoning Failures in LLMs Across Nine Complexity Dimensions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06799)
*Parth Patil,Dhruv Kumar,Yash Sinha,Murari Mandal*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a nine-dimensional framework to analyze algebraic reasoning failures in large language models (LLMs) independently across different complexity factors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks fail to attribute the cause of algebraic reasoning failure to specific complexity factors in LLMs, limiting diagnostic insights.

Method: The authors designed a parametric pipeline that automatically generates and verifies algebraic problems by varying one complexity factor at a time across nine documented dimensions of reasoning difficulty.

Result: Evaluation of seven LLMs shows that working memory is the dominant bottleneck, unaffected by model parameter scale, as all models fail with more than 20–30 parallel branches.

Conclusion: The study identifies five key dimensions across nine that provide sufficient diagnostic coverage for assessing algebraic reasoning capacity, highlighting critical architectural constraints in LLMs.

Abstract: Algebraic reasoning remains one of the most informative stress tests for large language models, yet current benchmarks provide no mechanism for attributing failure to a specific cause. When a model fails an algebraic problem, a single accuracy score cannot reveal whether the expression was too deeply nested, the operator too uncommon, the intermediate state count too high, or the dependency chain too long. Prior work has studied individual failure modes in isolation, but no framework has varied each complexity factor independently under strict experimental control. No prior system has offered automatic generation and verification of problems of increasing complexity to track model progress over time. We introduce a nine-dimension algebraic complexity framework in which each factor is varied independently while all others are held fixed, with problem generation and verification handled by a parametric pipeline requiring no human annotation. Each dimension is grounded in a documented LLM failure mode and captures a structurally distinct aspect of algebraic difficulty, including expression nesting depth, simultaneous intermediate result count, sub-expression complexity, operator hardness, and dependent reasoning chain length. We evaluated seven instruction-tuned models spanning 8B to 235B parameters across all nine dimensions and find that working memory is the dominant scale-invariant bottleneck. Every model collapses between 20 and 30 parallel branches regardless of parameter count, pointing to a hard architectural constraint rather than a solvable capacity limitation. Our analysis further identifies a minimal yet diagnostically sufficient subset of five dimensions that together span the full space of documented algebraic failure modes, providing a complete complexity profile of a model's algebraic reasoning capacity.

</details>


### [103] [Cognitive Loop of Thought: Reversible Hierarchical Markov Chain for Efficient Mathematical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06805)
*Jia-Chen Zhang,Zheng Zhou,Yu-Jie Xiong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces an enhanced CoT framework, Cognitive Loop of Thought (CLoT), for mathematical reasoning in LLMs, leveraging hierarchical dependencies and backward verification to overcome limitations in traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional Long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) models are computationally expensive and suffer from context loss and limited backward reasoning. The paper aims to address these issues for more efficient and robust reasoning.

Method: The paper proposes CLoT, a Reversible Hierarchical Markov Chain framework that integrates backward verification at hierarchical layers and employs a pruning strategy to reduce redundancy.

Result: CLoT shows superior performance, achieving 99.0% accuracy on the AddSub benchmark using GPT-4o-mini, outperforming traditional CoT methods by notable margins.

Conclusion: CLoT significantly enhances reasoning capabilities by mitigating error propagation, improving robustness, and optimizing computational efficiency.

Abstract: Multi-step Chain-of-Thought (CoT) has significantly advanced the mathematical reasoning capabilities of LLMs by leveraging explicit reasoning steps. However, the widespread adoption of Long CoT often results in sequence lengths that exceed manageable computational limits. While existing approaches attempt to alleviate this by reducing KV Cache redundancy via Markov chain-like structures, they introduce two critical limitations: inherent memorylessness (loss of context) and limited backward reasoning capability. To address these limitations, we propose a novel Chain-of-Thought framework based on Reversible Hierarchical Markov Chain, termed Cognitive Loop of Thought (CLoT), and a backward reasoning dataset CLoT-Instruct. In CLoT, problems are decomposed into sub-problems with hierarchical dependencies. Inspired by human cognitive processes, we introduce a backward verification mechanism at each hierarchical layer. Furthermore, we implement a pruning strategy: once higher-level sub-problems are verified, redundant lower-level sub-problems are pruned to maximize efficiency. This approach effectively mitigates error propagation and enhances reasoning robustness. Experiments on four mathematical benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our method. Notably, on the AddSub dataset using GPT-4o-mini, CLoT achieves 99.0% accuracy, outperforming traditional CoT and CoT-SC by 4.1% and 2.9%, respectively.

</details>


### [104] [AGSC: Adaptive Granularity and Semantic Clustering for Uncertainty Quantification in Long-text Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06812)
*Guanran Luo,Wentao Qiu,Wanru Zhao,Wenhan Lv,Zhongquan Jian,Meihong Wang,Qingqiang Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper addresses hallucination in long-form generation by presenting AGSC, a framework combining NLI-based irrelevance triggers and GMM soft clustering to improve uncertainty quantification and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to tackle the hallucination problem in long-form generation, which undermines the reliability of Large Language Models. Existing methods either lack computational efficiency or fail to handle neutral information nuances.

Method: The AGSC framework introduces NLI-neutral probabilities to identify irrelevant information and uses GMM-based semantic clustering to assign topic-aware weights for reliable aggregation, reducing unnecessary computations.

Result: AGSC demonstrates state-of-the-art correlation with factuality on BIO and LongFact datasets and achieves a 60% reduction in inference time compared to fine-grained atomic decomposition approaches.

Conclusion: AGSC provides an effective and efficient solution for uncertainty quantification in long-form generation while addressing existing challenges like irrelevance detection and computational overhead.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities in long-form generation, yet their application is hindered by the hallucination problem. While Uncertainty Quantification (UQ) is essential for assessing reliability, the complex structure makes reliable aggregation across heterogeneous themes difficult, in addition, existing methods often overlook the nuance of neutral information and suffer from the high computational cost of fine-grained decomposition. To address these challenges, we propose AGSC (Adaptive Granularity and GMM-based Semantic Clustering), a UQ framework tailored for long-form generation. AGSC first uses NLI neutral probabilities as triggers to distinguish irrelevance from uncertainty, reducing unnecessary computation. It then applies Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) soft clustering to model latent semantic themes and assign topic-aware weights for downstream aggregation. Experiments on BIO and LongFact show that AGSC achieves state-of-the-art correlation with factuality while reducing inference time by about 60% compared to full atomic decomposition.

</details>


### [105] [SemEval-2026 Task 9: Detecting Multilingual, Multicultural and Multievent Online Polarization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06817)
*Usman Naseem,Robert Geislinger,Juan Ren,Sarah Kohail,Rudy Garrido Veliz,P Sam Sahil,Yiran Zhang,Marco Antonio Stranisci,Idris Abdulmumin,Özge Alaçam,Cengiz Acartürk,Aisha Jabr,Saba Anwar,Abinew Ali Ayele,Elena Tutubalina,Aung Kyaw Htet,Xintong Wang,Surendrabikram Thapa,Tanmoy Chakraborty,Dheeraj Kodati,Sahar Moradizeyveh,Firoj Alam,Ye Kyaw Thu,Shantipriya Parida,Ihsan Ayyub Qazi,Lilian Wanzare,Nelson Odhiambo Onyango,Clemencia Siro,Ibrahim Said Ahmad,Adem Chanie Ali,Martin Semmann,Chris Biemann,Shamsuddeen Hassan Muhammad,Seid Muhie Yimam*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The study introduces SemEval-2026 Task 9, focusing on detecting online polarization across 22 languages using a dataset with over 110K instances. Participants predicted three aspects: polarization presence, type, and manifestation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the significant issue of online polarization by offering a multilingual shared task to detect and analyze polarization effectively.

Method: Participants worked on three subtasks: identifying the presence of polarization, its type, and its manifestation. A large labeled dataset was provided, and evaluation occurred on Codabench.

Result: Over 1,000 participants contributed 10k submissions, with 67 teams and 73 system papers providing results. Baseline and top-performing methods were analyzed for their effectiveness across languages and subtasks.

Conclusion: The task enabled significant progress in understanding and detecting polarization, with the dataset and best-performing systems providing valuable resources for further research.

Abstract: We present SemEval-2026 Task 9, a shared task on online polarization detection, covering 22 languages and comprising over 110K annotated instances. Each data instance is multi-labeled with the presence of polarization, polarization type, and polarization manifestation. Participants were asked to predict labels in three sub-tasks: (1) detecting the presence of polarization, (2) identifying the type of polarization, and (3) recognizing the polarization manifestation. The three tasks attracted over 1,000 participants worldwide and more than 10k submission on Codabench. We received final submissions from 67 teams and 73 system description papers. We report the baseline results and analyze the performance of the best-performing systems, highlighting the most common approaches and the most effective methods across different subtasks and languages. The dataset of this task is publicly available.

</details>


### [106] [Environmental, Social and Governance Sentiment Analysis on Slovene News: A Novel Dataset and Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06826)
*Paula Dodig,Boshko Koloski,Katarina Sitar Šuštar,Senja Pollak,Matthew Purver*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces the first Slovene ESG sentiment dataset, evaluates various machine learning models for ESG sentiment detection, and provides a case study using the best-performing classifiers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The lack of reliable ESG ratings for smaller companies and emerging markets motivated the authors to create a publicly available Slovene ESG sentiment dataset and explore effective automatic ESG sentiment detection.

Method: The dataset was created using LLM-assisted filtering combined with human annotations. The authors evaluated multiple model types, including monolingual and multilingual ones, embedding-based classifiers, hierarchical ensemble architectures, and large language models, using performance metrics such as F1-macro.

Result: LLMs showed the best performance overall but varied across ESG components: Gemma3-27B excelled in Environmental (F1-macro: 0.61), SloBERTa led in Governance (F1-macro: 0.54), and gpt-oss was best for Social aspects (F1-macro: 0.45).

Conclusion: The study highlights the utility of a Slovene ESG sentiment dataset and demonstrates the potential of various models, especially LLMs, to address gaps in ESG ratings for smaller firms and emerging markets.

Abstract: Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) considerations are increasingly integral to assessing corporate performance, reputation, and long-term sustainability. Yet, reliable ESG ratings remain limited for smaller companies and emerging markets. We introduce the first publicly available Slovene ESG sentiment dataset and a suite of models for automatic ESG sentiment detection. The dataset, derived from the MaCoCu Slovene news collection, combines large language model (LLM)-assisted filtering with human annotation of company-related ESG content. We evaluate the performance of monolingual (SloBERTa) and multilingual (XLM-R) models, embedding-based classifiers (TabPFN), hierarchical ensemble architectures, and large language models. Results show that LLMs achieve the strongest performance on Environmental (Gemma3-27B, F1-macro: 0.61) and Social aspects (gpt-oss 20B, F1-macro: 0.45), while fine-tuned SloBERTa is the best model on Governance classification (F1-macro: 0.54). We then show in a small case study how the best-preforming classifier (gpt-oss) can be applied to investigate ESG aspects for selected companies across a long time frame.

</details>


### [107] [WRAP++: Web discoveRy Amplified Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06829)
*Jiang Zhou,Yunhao Wang,Xing Wu,Tinghao Yu,Feng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: WRAP++ is introduced as a method to enhance LLM pretraining by synthesizing cross-document QA datasets, leveraging relational motifs and hyperlinks to amplify knowledge with contextual relationships.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing synthetic rephrasing approaches during LLM pretraining are limited to single-document analysis and miss cross-document relationships, which are crucial for comprehensive knowledge acquisition.

Method: WRAP++ discovers cross-document relationships using hyperlinks and relational motifs such as dual-links and co-mentions to synthesize QA pairs that require reasoning across document pairs.

Result: By applying WRAP++ on Wikipedia, raw text data of ~8.4B tokens is transformed into 80B tokens of cross-document QA datasets, enabling significant performance gains for LLMs (OLMo-based models) on benchmarks like SimpleQA.

Conclusion: Cross-document relationship synthesis and amplified associative context significantly improve large language models' knowledge acquisition and scaling efficiency during pretraining.

Abstract: Synthetic data rephrasing has emerged as a powerful technique for enhancing knowledge acquisition during large language model (LLM) pretraining. However, existing approaches operate at the single-document level, rewriting individual web pages in isolation. This confines synthesized examples to intra-document knowledge, missing cross-document relationships and leaving facts with limited associative context. We propose WRAP++ (Web discoveRy Amplified Pretraining), which amplifies the associative context of factual knowledge by discovering cross-document relationships from web hyperlinks and synthesizing joint QA over each discovered document pair. Concretely, WRAP++ discovers high-confidence relational motifs including dual-links and co-mentions, and synthesizes QA that requires reasoning across both documents. This produces relational knowledge absent from either source document alone, creating diverse entry points to the same facts. Because the number of valid entity pairs grows combinatorially, this discovery-driven synthesis also amplifies data scale far beyond single-document rewriting. Instantiating WRAP++ on Wikipedia, we amplify ~8.4B tokens of raw text into 80B tokens of cross-document QA data. On SimpleQA, OLMo-based models at both 7B and 32B scales trained with WRAP++ substantially outperform single-document approaches and exhibit sustained scaling gains, underscoring the advantage of cross-document knowledge discovery and amplification.

</details>


### [108] [Fast-dVLM: Efficient Block-Diffusion VLM via Direct Conversion from Autoregressive VLM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06832)
*Chengyue Wu,Shiyi Lan,Yonggan Fu,Sensen Gao,Jin Wang,Jincheng Yu,Jose M. Alvarez,Pavlo Molchanov,Ping Luo,Song Han,Ligeng Zhu,Enze Xie*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Fast-dVLM, a vision-language model using block-diffusion for parallel decoding. It significantly accelerates inference compared to traditional autoregressive models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLMs rely on autoregressive decoding, which is slow and underutilizes hardware parallelism, particularly in scenarios like robotics where edge devices operate under constrained conditions.

Method: Fast-dVLM employs block-diffusion and allows for parallel KV-cache-compatible and speculative block decoding. It integrates efficient conversion strategies, multimodal diffusion adaptations, and optimizations like block size annealing and FP8 quantization.

Result: Experiments across 11 multimodal benchmarks reveal comparable generation quality to autoregressive models, while achieving over 6x faster inference speed.

Conclusion: Fast-dVLM demonstrates that block-diffusion-based decoding is a viable and efficient alternative to autoregression for VLMs, making it more suitable for real-world edge applications.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) predominantly rely on autoregressive decoding, which generates tokens one at a time and fundamentally limits inference throughput. This limitation is especially acute in physical AI scenarios such as robotics and autonomous driving, where VLMs are deployed on edge devices at batch size one, making AR decoding memory-bandwidth-bound and leaving hardware parallelism underutilized. While block-wise discrete diffusion has shown promise for parallel text generation, extending it to VLMs remains challenging due to the need to jointly handle continuous visual representations and discrete text tokens while preserving pretrained multimodal capabilities. We present Fast-dVLM, a block-diffusion-based VLM that enables KV-cache-compatible parallel decoding and speculative block decoding for inference acceleration. We systematically compare two AR-to-diffusion conversion strategies: a two-stage approach that first adapts the LLM backbone with text-only diffusion fine-tuning before multimodal training, and a direct approach that converts the full AR VLM in one stage. Under comparable training budgets, direct conversion proves substantially more efficient by leveraging the already multimodally aligned VLM; we therefore adopt it as our recommended recipe. We introduce a suite of multimodal diffusion adaptations, block size annealing, causal context attention, auto-truncation masking, and vision efficient concatenation, that collectively enable effective block diffusion in the VLM setting. Extensive experiments across 11 multimodal benchmarks show Fast-dVLM matches its autoregressive counterpart in generation quality. With SGLang integration and FP8 quantization, Fast-dVLM achieves over 6x end-to-end inference speedup over the AR baseline.

</details>


### [109] [On the Step Length Confounding in LLM Reasoning Data Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06834)
*Bing Wang,Rui Miao,Chen Shen,Shaotian Yan,Kaiyuan Liu,Ximing Li,Xiaosong Yuan,Sinan Fan,Jun Zhang,Jieping Ye*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper identifies a bias in LLM reasoning dataset selection methods, where step length confounds quality assessment, and proposes debiasing methods to address it effectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to enhance the quality and reliability of reasoning datasets used to train Large Language Models (LLMs), addressing biases in selection methods that favor longer reasoning steps over truly high-quality samples.

Method: They propose two methods to reduce bias: (1) ASLEC-DROP, which excludes first-token probabilities from average log probability calculations, and (2) ASLEC-CASL, a causal debiasing regression that targets the confounding effect of first tokens.

Result: Experiments using four LLMs and five benchmarks show that the proposed methods effectively mitigate step length confounding, leading to improved dataset selection processes.

Conclusion: The proposed techniques successfully address a systematic bias in reasoning dataset selection, enhancing the quality of data available for fine-tuning LLMs and advancing reasoning task performance.

Abstract: Large reasoning models have recently demonstrated strong performance on complex tasks that require long chain-of-thought reasoning, through supervised fine-tuning on large-scale and high-quality datasets. To construct such datasets, existing pipelines generate long reasoning data from more capable Large Language Models (LLMs) and apply manually heuristic or naturalness-based selection methods to filter high-quality samples. Despite the proven effectiveness of naturalness-based data selection, which ranks data by the average log probability assigned by LLMs, our analysis shows that, when applied to LLM reasoning datasets, it systematically prefers samples with longer reasoning steps (i.e., more tokens per step) rather than higher-quality ones, a phenomenon we term step length confounding. Through quantitative analysis, we attribute this phenomenon to low-probability first tokens in reasoning steps; longer steps dilute their influence, thereby inflating the average log probabilities. To address this issue, we propose two variant methods: ASLEC-DROP, which drops first-token probabilities when computing average log probability, and ASLEC-CASL, which applies a causal debiasing regression to remove the first tokens' confounding effect. Experiments across four LLMs and five evaluation benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in mitigating the step length confounding problem.

</details>


### [110] [HingeMem: Boundary Guided Long-Term Memory with Query Adaptive Retrieval for Scalable Dialogues](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06845)
*Yijie Zhong,Yunfan Gao,Haofen Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: HingeMem introduces a novel approach to long-term memory for dialogue systems using interpretable event segmentation and query-adaptive retrieval, achieving significant efficiency and performance improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for long-term memory in dialogue systems are inefficient and lack adaptability across different queries, hindering continuous and personalized interactions.

Method: HingeMem uses event segmentation theory to create hyperedges over four elements (person, time, location, topic), triggers memory boundaries dynamically, and employs query-adaptive retrieval for optimal memory usage.

Result: HingeMem improves retrieval efficiency and achieves ~20% performance gains over baseline methods, while significantly reducing computational costs in token usage.

Conclusion: HingeMem provides a scalable and efficient memory solution for dialogue systems, making it suitable for real-world applications that require sustained and trustworthy interactions.

Abstract: Long-term memory is critical for dialogue systems that support continuous, sustainable, and personalized interactions. However, existing methods rely on continuous summarization or OpenIE-based graph construction paired with fixed Top-\textit{k} retrieval, leading to limited adaptability across query categories and high computational overhead. In this paper, we propose HingeMem, a boundary-guided long-term memory that operationalizes event segmentation theory to build an interpretable indexing interface via boundary-triggered hyperedges over four elements: person, time, location, and topic. When any such element changes, HingeMem draws a boundary and writes the current segment, thereby reducing redundant operations and preserving salient context. To enable robust and efficient retrieval under diverse information needs, HingeMem introduces query-adaptive retrieval mechanisms that jointly decide (a) \textit{what to retrieve}: determine the query-conditioned routing over the element-indexed memory; (b) \textit{how much to retrieve}: control the retrieval depth based on the estimated query type. Extensive experiments across LLM scales (from 0.6B to production-tier models; \textit{e.g.}, Qwen3-0.6B to Qwen-Flash) on LOCOMO show that HingeMem achieves approximately $20\%$ relative improvement over strong baselines without query categories specification, while reducing computational cost (68\%$\downarrow$ question answering token cost compared to HippoRAG2). Beyond advancing memory modeling, HingeMem's adaptive retrieval makes it a strong fit for web applications requiring efficient and trustworthy memory over extended interactions.

</details>


### [111] [MedDialBench: Benchmarking LLM Diagnostic Robustness under Parametric Adversarial Patient Behaviors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06846)
*Xiaotian Luo,Xun Jiang,Jiangcheng Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: MedDialBench introduces a controlled benchmark to assess how different dimensions of patient non-cooperative behavior affect diagnostic accuracy of large language models (LLMs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks fail to analyze graded severity, case-specific grounding, and cross-dimension interactions for patient non-cooperation during medical dialogues.

Method: MedDialBench decomposes patient behavior into five graded dimensions, enabling factorial design for sensitivity analysis, dose-response profiling, and interaction detection.

Result: Analysis of 7,225 dialogues showed fabricated symptoms caused significantly larger accuracy drops (1.7-3.4x) compared to withheld information. Fabricated inputs were sole drivers of super-additive interactions across behavioral dimensions.

Conclusion: LLMs exhibit diverse vulnerabilities to patient behaviors; exhaustive questioning recovers withheld details but fails against fabricated data, highlighting diagnostic robustness challenges.

Abstract: Interactive medical dialogue benchmarks have shown that LLM diagnostic accuracy degrades significantly when interacting with non-cooperative patients, yet existing approaches either apply adversarial behaviors without graded severity or case-specific grounding, or reduce patient non-cooperation to a single ungraded axis, and none analyze cross-dimension interactions.
  We introduce MedDialBench, a benchmark enabling controlled, dose-response characterization of how individual patient behavior dimensions affect LLM diagnostic robustness. It decomposes patient behavior into five dimensions -- Logic Consistency, Health Cognition, Expression Style, Disclosure, and Attitude -- each with graded severity levels and case-specific behavioral scripts. This controlled factorial design enables graded sensitivity analysis, dose-response profiling, and cross-dimension interaction detection.
  Evaluating five frontier LLMs across 7,225 dialogues (85 cases x 17 configurations x 5 models), we find a fundamental asymmetry: information pollution (fabricating symptoms) produces 1.7-3.4x larger accuracy drops than information deficit (withholding information), and fabricating is the only configuration achieving statistical significance across all five models (McNemar p < 0.05). Among six dimension combinations, fabricating is the sole driver of super-additive interaction: all three fabricating-involving pairs produce O/E ratios of 0.70-0.81 (35-44% of eligible cases fail under the combination despite succeeding under each dimension alone), while all non-fabricating pairs show purely additive effects (O/E ~ 1.0). Inquiry strategy moderates deficit but not pollution: exhaustive questioning recovers withheld information, but cannot compensate for fabricated inputs. Models exhibit distinct vulnerability profiles, with worst-case drops ranging from 38.8 to 54.1 percentage points.

</details>


### [112] [To Adapt or not to Adapt, Rethinking the Value of Medical Knowledge-Aware Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06854)
*Ane G. Domingo-Aldama,Iker De La Iglesia,Maitane Urruela,Aitziber Atutxa,Ander Barrena*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper examines how well clinical versus general-purpose large language models perform on diverse medical tasks. It concludes that specialized clinical models offer limited and inconsistent improvements over general models in English, but newer models like Marmoka perform better in Spanish.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper seeks to address whether specialized clinical adaptation significantly enhances large language models on standard medical benchmarks, challenging prior assumptions.

Method: The study evaluates general and clinical LLMs on medical question-answering tasks in English and Spanish using standard and newly proposed benchmarks. It introduces the 8B-parameter Marmoka models for clinical tasks, exploring robustness and adversarial sensitivity.

Result: Clinical models did not consistently outperform general-purpose models on English tasks but achieved better results in Spanish, particularly with the new Marmoka models.

Conclusion: Clinical adaptation provides limited improvements in English tasks under existing benchmarks. However, more robust medical LLMs can be developed for low-resource languages like Spanish, as evidenced by the performance of Marmoka models.

Abstract: BACKGROUND: Recent studies have shown that domain-adapted large language models (LLMs) do not consistently outperform general-purpose counterparts on standard medical benchmarks, raising questions about the need for specialized clinical adaptation.
  METHODS: We systematically compare general and clinical LLMs on a diverse set of multiple choice clinical question answering tasks in English and Spanish. We introduce a perturbation based evaluation benchmark that probes model robustness, instruction following, and sensitivity to adversarial variations. Our evaluation includes, one-step and two-step question transformations, multi prompt testing and instruction guided assessment. We analyze a range of state-of-the-art clinical models and their general-purpose counterparts, focusing on Llama 3.1-based models. Additionally, we introduce Marmoka, a family of lightweight 8B-parameter clinical LLMs for English and Spanish, developed via continual domain-adaptive pretraining on medical corpora and instructions.
  RESULTS: The experiments show that clinical LLMs do not consistently outperform their general purpose counterparts on English clinical tasks, even under the proposed perturbation based benchmark. However, for the Spanish subsets the proposed Marmoka models obtain better results compared to Llama.
  CONCLUSIONS: Our results show that, under current short-form MCQA benchmarks, clinical LLMs offer only marginal and unstable improvements over general-purpose models in English, suggesting that existing evaluation frameworks may be insufficient to capture genuine medical expertise. We further find that both general and clinical models exhibit substantial limitations in instruction following and strict output formatting. Finally, we demonstrate that robust medical LLMs can be successfully developed for low-resource languages such as Spanish, as evidenced by the Marmoka models.

</details>


### [113] [Do We Need Distinct Representations for Every Speech Token? Unveiling and Exploiting Redundancy in Large Speech Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06871)
*Bajian Xiang,Tingwei Guo,Xuan Chen,Yang Han*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces Affinity Pooling, a mechanism that compresses speech language model representations by leveraging redundancy in deep layers, reducing computational costs and maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the inefficiency of large speech language models caused by their high token rates and long sequence processing.

Method: Layer-wise oracle interventions reveal redundancy in deep layers, and Affinity Pooling is proposed as a training-free, similarity-based token merging mechanism applied to both input and deep layers.

Result: The method reduces prefilling FLOPs by 27.48%, enables up to ∼1.7× memory savings, and ∼1.1× faster time-to-first-token while maintaining competitive accuracy.

Conclusion: Affinity Pooling challenges the necessity of distinct token representations, offering efficiency improvements and new perspectives on large speech language model design.

Abstract: Large Speech Language Models (LSLMs) typically operate at high token rates (tokens/s) to ensure acoustic fidelity, yet this results in sequence lengths that far exceed the underlying semantic content, incurring prohibitive inference costs. In this paper, we empirically revisit the necessity of such granular token-level processing. Through layer-wise oracle interventions, we unveil a structured redundancy hierarchy: while shallow layers encode essential acoustic details, deep layers exhibit extreme redundancy, allowing for aggressive compression. Motivated by these findings, we introduce Affinity Pooling, a training-free, similarity-based token merging mechanism. By strategically applying this method at both input and deep layers, we effectively compress speech representations without compromising semantic information. Extensive evaluations across three tasks demonstrate that our approach reduces prefilling FLOPs by 27.48\% while maintaining competitive accuracy. Practical deployment further confirms significant efficiency gains, yielding up to $\sim$1.7$\times$ memory savings and $\sim$1.1$\times$ faster time-to-first-token on long utterances. Our results challenge the necessity of fully distinct token representations, providing new perspectives on LSLM efficiency.

</details>


### [114] [iTAG: Inverse Design for Natural Text Generation with Accurate Causal Graph Annotations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06902)
*Wenshuo Wang,Boyu Cao,Nan Zhuang,Wei Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces iTAG, a method for generating causally annotated text data while balancing naturalness and annotation accuracy, addressing the lack of such datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a significant need for causally annotated text datasets to aid causal discovery from text, but creating them is costly. Existing generation methods either compromise text naturalness or fail to guarantee annotation accuracy.

Method: The proposed iTAG method combines real-world concept assignment and iterative Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning. It frames the task as an inverse problem, ensuring generated text aligns with the causal relationships in the target graph.

Result: iTAG achieves high annotation accuracy and naturalness in generated data, and its use in testing causal discovery algorithms shows strong correlations with real-world datasets.

Conclusion: iTAG provides a scalable and practical solution for generating text with causal annotations, enabling effective benchmarking of causal discovery algorithms and bridging gaps in available datasets.

Abstract: A fundamental obstacle to causal discovery from text is the lack of causally annotated text data for use as ground truth, due to high annotation costs. This motivates an important task of generating text with causal graph annotations. Early template-based generation methods sacrifice text naturalness in exchange for high causal graph annotation accuracy. Recent Large Language Model (LLM)-dependent methods directly generate natural text from target graphs through LLMs, but do not guarantee causal graph annotation accuracy. Therefore, we propose iTAG, which performs real-world concept assignment to nodes before converting causal graphs into text in existing LLM-dependent methods. iTAG frames this process as an inverse problem with the causal graph as the target, iteratively examining and refining concept selection through Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning so that the induced relations between concepts are as consistent as possible with the target causal relationships described by the causal graph. iTAG demonstrates both extremely high annotation accuracy and naturalness across extensive tests, and the results of testing text-based causal discovery algorithms with the generated data show high statistical correlation with real-world data. This suggests that iTAG-generated data can serve as a practical surrogate for scalable benchmarking of text-based causal discovery algorithms.

</details>


### [115] [Is Biomedical Specialization Still Worth It? Insights from Domain-Adaptive Language Modelling with a New French Health Corpus](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06903)
*Aidan Mannion,Cécile Macaire,Armand Violle,Stéphane Ohayon,Xavier Tannier,Didier Schwab,Lorraine Goeuriot,François Portet*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The study examines specialized domain adaptation for small to mid-sized French biomedical LLMs using domain-adaptive pre-training (DAPT). It questions the efficacy of DAPT while highlighting its potential in resource-constrained settings with proper optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The research aims to improve the adaptability of language models to specialized fields like French biomedical contexts, addressing challenges in non-English domains.

Method: The authors used collected high-quality French biomedical texts for DAPT with causal language modeling approaches, followed by comprehensive evaluations.

Result: The findings question the effectiveness of DAPT overall but show its potential utility in smaller-scale setups. The study also highlights the importance of post-DAPT model merging to balance generalization and specialization.

Conclusion: DAPT may not universally enhance domain-specific performance but can be effective in targeted, resource-limited cases when combined with strategies like model merging. This approach can mitigate general capability degradation and potentially improve specialized task performance.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across diverse domains, yet their adaptation to specialized fields remains challenging, particularly for non-English languages. This study investigates domain-adaptive pre-training (DAPT) as a strategy for specializing small to mid-sized LLMs in the French biomedical domain through continued pre-training. We address two key research questions: the viability of specialized continued pre-training for domain adaptation and the relationship between domain-specific performance gains and general capability degradation. Our contributions include the release of a fully open-licensed French biomedical corpus suitable for commercial and open-source applications, the training and release of specialized French biomedical LLMs, and novel insights for DAPT implementation. Our methodology encompasses the collection and refinement of high-quality French biomedical texts, the exploration of causal language modeling approaches using DAPT, and conducting extensive comparative evaluations. Our results cast doubt on the efficacy of DAPT, in contrast to previous works, but we highlight its viability in smaller-scale, resource-constrained scenarios under the right conditions. Findings in this paper further suggest that model merging post-DAPT is essential to mitigate generalization trade-offs, and in some cases even improves performance on specialized tasks at which the DAPT was directed.

</details>


### [116] [The AI Skills Shift: Mapping Skill Obsolescence, Emergence, and Transition Pathways in the LLM Era](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06906)
*Rudra Jadhav,Janhavi Danve*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes the Skill Automation Feasibility Index (SAFI) to measure how susceptible occupational skills are to automation by benchmarking four top-tier Large Language Models (LLMs) against various tasks. Key findings include differences in automation feasibility across skills and a tendency for most AI interactions to support augmentation rather than automation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to provide data-driven insights into which occupational skills are most prone to automation, helping policymakers and workers address challenges brought on by advancements in large language models (LLMs).

Method: The authors benchmarked four frontier LLMs (LLaMA 3.3 70B, Mistral Large, Qwen 2.5 72B, Gemini 2.5 Flash) on 263 tasks across 35 skills in the U.S. Department of Labor's O*NET taxonomy. They cross-referenced findings with real-world AI adoption data and introduced the AI Impact Matrix for categorizing skill susceptibility.

Result: (1) Skills like Mathematics and Programming showed the highest feasibility for automation, while skills like Active Listening and Reading Comprehension ranked lowest. (2) Notable capability-demand inversion observed, where skills in AI-exposed jobs are often those LLMs struggle with. (3) 78.7% of AI applications were augmentation-focused. (4) Automation feasibility scores across models varied minimally, indicating skill dependency over model dependency.

Conclusion: Automation feasibility is highly skill-specific rather than model-specific, with most AI applications facilitating human work augmentation rather than outright replacement. The SAFI tool and AI Impact Matrix offer valuable frameworks for gauging AI's impact across skills. All supporting data are open-sourced for further analysis.

Abstract: As Large Language Models reshape the global labor market, policymakers and workers need empirical data on which occupational skills may be most susceptible to automation. We present the Skill Automation Feasibility Index (SAFI), benchmarking four frontier LLMs -- LLaMA 3.3 70B, Mistral Large, Qwen 2.5 72B, and Gemini 2.5 Flash -- across 263 text-based tasks spanning all 35 skills in the U.S. Department of Labor's O*NET taxonomy (1,052 total model calls, 0% failure rate). Cross-referencing with real-world AI adoption data from the Anthropic Economic Index (756 occupations, 17,998 tasks), we propose an AI Impact Matrix -- an interpretive framework that positions skills along four quadrants: High Displacement Risk, Upskilling Required, AI-Augmented, and Lower Displacement Risk. Key findings: (1) Mathematics (SAFI: 73.2) and Programming (71.8) receive the highest automation feasibility scores; Active Listening (42.2) and Reading Comprehension (45.5) receive the lowest; (2) a "capability-demand inversion" where skills most demanded in AI-exposed jobs are those LLMs perform least well at in our benchmark; (3) 78.7% of observed AI interactions are augmentation, not automation; (4) all four models converge to similar skill profiles (3.6-point spread), suggesting that text-based automation feasibility may be more skill-dependent than model-dependent. SAFI measures LLM performance on text-based representations of skills, not full occupational execution. All data, code, and model responses are open-sourced.

</details>


### [117] [Self-Preference Bias in Rubric-Based Evaluation of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06996)
*José Pombal,Ricardo Rei,André F. T. Martins*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper examines self-preference bias (SPB) in rubric-based evaluations where LLMs tend to favor their own outputs, causing skewed assessments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bias in LLM-based evaluation hinders objective benchmarking, particularly affecting recursive self-improvement and model development.

Method: The study uses IFEval for objective rubrics and HealthBench for subjective ones while introducing ensembling techniques to address SPB.

Result: SPB is persistent, leading to 50% error in objective rubrics and 10-point score distortions in subjective rubrics, even when mitigated.

Conclusion: SPB significantly affects LLM evaluation, and while ensembling mitigates bias, it doesn't fully eliminate it, necessitating further countermeasures.

Abstract: LLM-as-a-judge has become the de facto approach for evaluating LLM outputs. However, judges are known to exhibit self-preference bias (SPB): they tend to favor outputs produced by themselves or by models from their own family. This skews evaluations and, thus, hinders model development, especially in settings of recursive self-improvement. We present the first study of SPB in rubric-based evaluation, an increasingly popular benchmarking paradigm where judges issue binary verdicts on individual evaluation criteria, instead of assigning holistic scores or rankings. Using IFEval, a benchmark with programmatically verifiable rubrics, we show that SPB persists even when evaluation criteria are entirely objective: among rubrics where generators fail, judges can be up to 50\% more likely to incorrectly mark them as satisfied when the output is their own. We also find that, similarly to other evaluation paradigms, ensembling multiple judges helps mitigate SPB, but without fully eliminating it. On HealthBench, a medical chat benchmark with subjective rubrics, we observe that SPB skews model scores by up to 10 points, a potentially decisive margin when ranking frontier models. We analyze the factors that drive SPB in this setting, finding that negative rubrics, extreme rubric lengths, and subjective topics like emergency referrals are particularly susceptible.

</details>


### [118] [ChunQiuTR: Time-Keyed Temporal Retrieval in Classical Chinese Annals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06997)
*Yihao Wang,Zijian He,Jie Ren,Keze Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents ChunQiuTR, a benchmark for time-aware retrieval in Classical Chinese historical texts, and a new time-aware dual-encoder model, CTD, to address temporal consistency issues in retrieval-augmented generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from challenges in retrieving temporally consistent records, not just topically relevant ones, especially in complex historical documents like Classical Chinese annals where time expressions are implicit and non-standard.

Method: The authors created ChunQiuTR, a benchmark dataset with month-level reign keys, and proposed CTD, a dual-encoder that incorporates calendrical temporal context (absolute and relative offsets) to improve temporal consistency in retrieval tasks.

Result: Experiments demonstrated that CTD outperformed strong semantic dual-encoder baselines in time-keyed evaluations, improving temporal consistency in retrieval tasks.

Conclusion: The study concludes that temporal consistency during retrieval is crucial for effective historical research and demonstrates the utility of ChunQiuTR and the CTD model for advancing retrieval-augmented generation in such contexts.

Abstract: Retrieval shapes how language models access and ground knowledge in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). In historical research, the target is often not an arbitrary relevant passage, but the exact record for a specific regnal month, where temporal consistency matters as much as topical relevance. This is especially challenging for Classical Chinese annals, where time is expressed through terse, implicit, non-Gregorian reign phrases that must be interpreted from surrounding context, so semantically plausible evidence can still be temporally invalid. We introduce \textbf{ChunQiuTR}, a time-keyed retrieval benchmark built from the \textit{Spring and Autumn Annals} and its exegetical tradition. ChunQiuTR organizes records by month-level reign keys and includes chrono-near confounders that mirror realistic retrieval failures. We further propose \textbf{CTD} (Calendrical Temporal Dual-encoder), a time-aware dual-encoder that combines Fourier-based absolute calendrical context with relative offset biasing. Experiments show consistent gains over strong semantic dual-encoder baselines under time-keyed evaluation, supporting retrieval-time temporal consistency as a key prerequisite for faithful downstream historical RAG. Our code and datasets are available at \href{https://github.com/xbdxwyh/ChunQiuTR}{\texttt{github.com/xbdxwyh/ChunQiuTR}}.

</details>


### [119] [Continuous Interpretive Steering for Scalar Diversity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07006)
*Ye-eun Cho*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a method, Continuous Interpretive Steering (CIS), to probe graded pragmatic inference in language models using activation-level manipulations. Along with a new dataset, GraSD, it evaluates how LLMs handle scalar diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pragmatic inference in language models, especially scalar implicatures, lacks systematic assessment beyond prompt-level manipulations. The study aims to explore how pragmatic sensitivity can be precisely evaluated at a graded level.

Method: Authors introduce CIS, which tests pragmatic interpretations as a continuous experimental variable by steering activation levels. They pair this with a dataset, GraSD, encoding scalar diversity for experimentation.

Result: Experiments showed that uniform activation steering globally increased pragmatic interpretations but erased item-level variation. In contrast, graded activation steering preserved scalar diversity properties, recovering systematic representation of graded sensitivity.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that LLMs encode graded pragmatic sensitivity in their latent spaces and offers tools—CIS and GraSD—for controlled, systematic evaluation of such phenomena.

Abstract: Pragmatic inference is inherently graded. Different lexical items give rise to pragmatic enrichment to different degrees. Scalar implicature exemplifies this property through scalar diversity, where implicature strength varies across scalar items. However, evaluations of pragmatic inference in large language models (LLMs) often rely on prompt-based manipulations. Beyond prompt-level effects, this study introduces Continuous Interpretive Steering (CIS), a method that probes graded pragmatic interpretation by treating activation-level steering strength as a continuous experimental variable. To support this analysis, this study introduces a new dataset, GraSD, which encodes graded scalar diversity. Experiments on four LLMs show that uniform activation steering increases pragmatic interpretations globally but collapses item-level variation, whereas graded activation steering yields differentiated interpretive shifts aligned with scalar diversity grades. It indicates that graded sensitivity is encoded in the representation space and can be systematically recovered through controlled intervention. Together, CIS and GraSD provide a principled framework for evaluating graded pragmatic sensitivity in LLMs.

</details>


### [120] [DTCRS: Dynamic Tree Construction for Recursive Summarization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07012)
*Guanran Luo,Zhongquan Jian,Wentao Qiu,Meihong Wang,Qingqiang Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: DTCRS dynamically generates summary trees based on document structure and query semantics to improve large language models' question-answering abilities with reduced construction time and improved relevance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aimed to mitigate issues with existing recursive summarization methods, such as redundant nodes and inefficiency, while also enhancing the utility of this summarization for diverse question types.

Method: DTCRS analyzes the question type, decomposes the query, and utilizes sub-question embeddings as initial cluster centers. This process integrates document structure and semantics to reduce redundancy and improve QA performance.

Result: DTCRS cuts summary tree construction time and significantly enhances performance across three QA tasks. It also provides insights into how recursive summarization applies to different question types.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that DTCRS efficiently addresses redundancy in recursive summarization, enhances question-answering accuracy, and provides a robust framework for understanding the interaction between summary trees and question types.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) mitigates the hallucination problem of Large Language Models (LLMs) by incorporating external knowledge. Recursive summarization constructs a hierarchical summary tree by clustering text chunks, integrating information from multiple parts of a document to provide evidence for abstractive questions involving multi-step reasoning. However, summary trees often contain a large number of redundant summary nodes, which not only increase construction time but may also negatively impact question answering. Moreover, recursive summarization is not suitable for all types of questions. We introduce DTCRS, a method that dynamically generates summary trees based on document structure and query semantics.
