Under subject-independent leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) evaluation, the full multimodal model achieved balanced accuracy/F1 of 0.68 +/- 0.08/0.67 +/- 0.09 at M3 and 0.70 +/- 0.10/0.69 +/- 0.08 at M6 for handgrip, and 0.59 +/- 0.04/0.58 +/- 0.06 at M3 and 0.64 +/- 0.05/0.63 +/- 0.07 at M6 for FACIT-F. Ablation results indicated that smartwatch activity and sleep provide the strongest predictive information for frailty-related functional changes, while HRV contributes complementary information when fused with smartwatch streams.

</details>


### [327] [Stress Estimation in Elderly Oncology Patients Using Visual Wearable Representations and Multi-Instance Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06990)
*Ioannis Kyprakis,Vasileios Skaramagkas,Georgia Karanasiou,Vasilis Bouratzis,Andri Papakonstantinou,Dimitar Stefanovski,Kalliopi Keramida,Aristofania Simatou,Ketti Mazzocco,Anastasia Constantinidou,Konstantinos Marias,Dimitrios I. Fotiadis,Manolis Tsiknakis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study estimates perceived stress in elderly breast cancer patients using multimodal wearable data, predicting stress scores using a weakly supervised machine learning approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To integrate psychological stress assessment into continuous cardiotoxicity surveillance for cancer patients, enhancing traditional PROMs with wearable data.

Method: The researchers use smartwatch and ECG data to create visual representations analyzed via a lightweight pretrained model (Tiny-BioMoE) and attention-based MIL to predict stress scores.

Result: Predictions demonstrated moderate accuracy with questionnaire scores (e.g., M3 Pearson r=0.42, M6 Pearson r=0.49), showcasing wearable streams’ potential.

Conclusion: Wearable data and machine learning provide a promising avenue for stress monitoring in cardio-oncology, but further refinement is needed for greater accuracy.

Abstract: Psychological stress is clinically relevant in cardio-oncology, yet it is typically assessed only through patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) and is rarely integrated into continuous cardiotoxicity surveillance. We estimate perceived stress in an elderly, multicenter breast cancer cohort (CARDIOCARE) using multimodal wearable data from a smartwatch (physical activity and sleep) and a chest-worn ECG sensor. Wearable streams are transformed into heterogeneous visual representations, yielding a weakly supervised setting in which a single Perceived Stress Scale (PSS) score corresponds to many unlabeled windows. A lightweight pretrained mixture-of-experts backbone (Tiny-BioMoE) embeds each representation into 192-dimensional vectors, which are aggregated via attention-based multiple instance learning (MIL) to predict PSS at month 3 (M3) and month 6 (M6). Under leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) evaluation, predictions showed moderate agreement with questionnaire scores (M3: R^2=0.24, Pearson r=0.42, Spearman rho=0.48; M6: R^2=0.28, Pearson r=0.49, Spearman rho=0.52), with global RMSE/MAE of 6.62/6.07 at M3 and 6.13/5.54 at M6.

</details>


### [328] [Predictive Representations for Skill Transfer in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07016)
*Ruben Vereecken,Luke Dickens,Alessandra Russo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Outcome-Predictive State Representations (OPSRs), a framework for transferable state abstraction and skills in reinforcement learning, enabling faster learning in unseen tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge in reinforcement learning where agents lack the ability to generalize learned behaviors and instead relearn each task from scratch.

Method: The authors propose a framework called OPSRs, which are compact, task-independent state abstractions based on outcome predictions. They further develop OPSR-based skills—reusable abstract actions derived from state abstractions. These are learned from demonstrations and used for transfer between tasks.

Result: OPSR-based skills are shown empirically to significantly speed up learning in new and unseen tasks, eliminating the need for pre-processing.

Conclusion: The paper provides a promising framework that advances transfer learning in reinforcement learning through the combination of state and action abstraction, potentially enabling efficient generalization across tasks.

Abstract: A key challenge in scaling up Reinforcement Learning is generalizing learned behaviour. Without the ability to carry forward acquired knowledge an agent is doomed to learn each task from scratch. In this paper we develop a new formalism for transfer by virtue of state abstraction. Based on task-independent, compact observations (outcomes) of the environment, we introduce Outcome-Predictive State Representations (OPSRs), agent-centered and task-independent abstractions that are made up of predictions of outcomes. We show formally and empirically that they have the potential for optimal but limited transfer, then overcome this trade-off by introducing OPSR-based skills, i.e. abstract actions (based on options) that can be reused between tasks as a result of state abstraction. In a series of empirical studies, we learn OPSR-based skills from demonstrations and show how they speed up learning considerably in entirely new and unseen tasks without any pre-processing. We believe that the framework introduced in this work is a promising step towards transfer in RL in general, and towards transfer through combining state and action abstraction specifically.

</details>


### [329] [ConceptTracer: Interactive Analysis of Concept Saliency and Selectivity in Neural Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07019)
*Ricardo Knauer,Andre Beinrucker,Erik Rodner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces ConceptTracer, a tool that aids in understanding neural network representations using an information-theoretic approach to analyze human-interpretable concepts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of opacity in neural network decision-making and the lack of tools for exploring learned representations, particularly in tabular foundation models.

Method: The authors propose ConceptTracer, an interactive application that uses information-theoretic measures—concept saliency and selectivity—to identify neurons linked to specific human-interpretable concepts.

Result: The method was tested on representations learned by TabPFN, demonstrating its ability to uncover interpretable neurons effectively.

Conclusion: ConceptTracer provides a functional framework for investigating concept-level encoding in neural networks, offering a systematic and practical approach for researchers and practitioners.

Abstract: Neural networks deliver impressive predictive performance across a variety of tasks, but they are often opaque in their decision-making processes. Despite a growing interest in mechanistic interpretability, tools for systematically exploring the representations learned by neural networks in general, and tabular foundation models in particular, remain limited. In this work, we introduce ConceptTracer, an interactive application for analyzing neural representations through the lens of human-interpretable concepts. ConceptTracer integrates two information-theoretic measures that quantify concept saliency and selectivity, enabling researchers and practitioners to identify neurons that respond strongly to individual concepts. We demonstrate the utility of ConceptTracer on representations learned by TabPFN and show that our approach facilitates the discovery of interpretable neurons. Together, these capabilities provide a practical framework for investigating how neural networks like TabPFN encode concept-level information. ConceptTracer is available at https://github.com/ml-lab-htw/concept-tracer.

</details>


### [330] [Learning to Query History: Nonstationary Classification via Learned Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07027)
*Jimmy Gammell,Bishal Thapaliya,Yoon Jung,Riyasat Ohib,Bilel Fehri,Deepayan Chakrabarti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a solution for handling nonstationary classification tasks by reformulating the problem as time series prediction and introducing a discrete retrieval mechanism for scalable learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the poor performance of classification models under nonstationary conditions, where data distributions change over time, despite models performing well on holdout sets during training.

Method: The method involves conditioning the classifier on a sequence of historical labeled examples. A learned discrete retrieval mechanism is introduced to sample relevant historical data using input-dependent queries. This process is trained end-to-end with a score-based gradient estimator, allowing efficient retrieval from a large historical corpus stored on external filesystems.

Result: The proposed approach demonstrates robustness to distribution shifts on synthetic benchmarks and the Amazon Reviews '23 dataset, with predictable VRAM scaling as historical sequence length increases.

Conclusion: The approach provides a scalable and robust solution for nonstationary classification tasks, leveraging historical data effectively to address distribution shifts.

Abstract: Nonstationarity is ubiquitous in practical classification settings, leading deployed models to perform poorly even when they generalize well to holdout sets available at training time. We address this by reframing nonstationary classification as time series prediction: rather than predicting from the current input alone, we condition the classifier on a sequence of historical labeled examples that extends beyond the training cutoff. To scale to large sequences, we introduce a learned discrete retrieval mechanism that samples relevant historical examples via input-dependent queries, trained end-to-end with the classifier using a score-based gradient estimator. This enables the full corpus of historical data to remain on an arbitrary filesystem during training and deployment. Experiments on synthetic benchmarks and Amazon Reviews '23 (electronics category) show improved robustness to distribution shift compared to standard classifiers, with VRAM scaling predictably as the length of the historical data sequence increases.

</details>


### [331] [MoE Routing Testbed: Studying Expert Specialization and Routing Behavior at Small Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07030)
*Tobias Falke,Nicolas Anastassacos,Samson Tan,Chankrisna Richy Meas,Chandana Satya Prakash,Nitesh Sekhar,M Saiful Bari,Krishna Kompella,Gamaleldin F. Elsayed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a methodology to assess and improve routing dynamics in Sparse Mixture-of-Experts models for large language models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts architectures face challenges in training due to routing complexity, with a need for metrics to assess expert specialization and utilization.

Method: The authors propose the MoE Routing Testbed, a setup combining distinct domain data with a reference router to quantify expert specialization and compare routing techniques.

Result: The study demonstrates the importance of balancing scope for achieving specialization and high utilization, validated across models up to 35x larger.

Conclusion: The introduced testbed provides visibility into routing dynamics and establishes a foundation for better performance evaluation in MoE-based language models.

Abstract: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures are increasingly popular for frontier large language models (LLM) but they introduce training challenges due to routing complexity. Fully leveraging parameters of an MoE model requires all experts to be well-trained and to specialize in non-redundant ways. Assessing this, however, is complicated due to lack of established metrics and, importantly, many routing techniques exhibit similar performance at smaller sizes, which is often not reflective of their behavior at large scale. To address this challenge, we propose the MoE Routing Testbed, a setup that gives clearer visibility into routing dynamics at small scale while using realistic data. The testbed pairs a data mix with clearly distinguishable domains with a reference router that prescribes ideal routing based on these domains, providing a well-defined upper bound for comparison. This enables quantifiable measurement of expert specialization. To demonstrate the value of the testbed, we compare various MoE routing approaches and show that balancing scope is the crucial factor that allows specialization while maintaining high expert utilization. We confirm that this observation generalizes to models 35x larger.

</details>


### [332] [AdaBoost Does Not Always Cycle: A Computer-Assisted Counterexample](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07055)
*Erik Y. Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A counterexample demonstrates that exhaustive AdaBoost does not always converge to a finite cycle, refuting an open question by Rudin, Schapire, and Daubechies using a novel construction with rigorous mathematical analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper seeks to address an open question from COLT 2012 about whether exhaustive AdaBoost always converges to a finite cycle, which has implications for understanding the behavior and limitations of this machine learning algorithm.

Method: The authors use a block-product gadget construction with shared period-2 orbits and analyze the system's dynamics using exact rational arithmetic and eigenvalue calculations, showing the presence of irrational asymptotic frequency.

Result: They present a rigorously verified counterexample where the AdaBoost system fails to settle into an eventual periodic cycle due to unique dynamical properties of their constructed system.

Conclusion: The findings conclusively disprove the conjecture that exhaustive AdaBoost invariably converges to a finite cycle, highlighting fundamental limitations of the algorithm's dynamics.

Abstract: We give a computer-assisted counterexample to the open question, posed by Rudin, Schapire, and Daubechies in COLT 2012, of whether exhaustive AdaBoost always converges to a finite cycle. The construction is based on a block-product gadget whose two factors share an exact period-2 orbit for their 5-step branch maps, but whose linearized return maps have dominant eigenvalues with an irrational logarithmic ratio. This irrationality forces the burst-winner sequence to have an irrational asymptotic frequency, precluding eventual periodicity. All assertions are certified by exact rational arithmetic. This work was developed in collaboration with GPT-5.4 Pro and Claude Opus 4.6.

</details>


### [333] [Production-Ready Automated ECU Calibration using Residual Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07059)
*Andreas Kampmeier,Kevin Badalian,Lucas Koch,Sung-Yong Lee,Jakob Andert*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes an explainable method using residual reinforcement learning (RL) to automate ECU calibration, improving efficiency and reducing time while adhering to automotive development standards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from challenges faced by traditional manual ECU calibration methods due to increasing customer expectations, stricter emissions standards, and the need for shorter product development cycles, all of which render conventional approaches impractical and costly.

Method: The paper employs residual reinforcement learning to automate ECU calibration. The approach follows established automotive development principles and was validated using a map-based air path controller on a hardware-in-the-loop (HiL) platform.

Result: The proposed methodology quickly converges to a calibration that closely matches the reference in the ECU, demonstrating its utility in producing superior calibrations in significantly less time with minimal human input.

Conclusion: The method is effective for industrial use, offering explainable and efficient calibration processes that align with industry standards, enabling better performance and reduced development costs.

Abstract: Electronic Control Units (ECUs) have played a pivotal role in transforming motorcars of yore into the modern vehicles we see on our roads today. They actively regulate the actuation of individual components and thus determine the characteristics of the whole system. In this, the behavior of the control functions heavily depends on their calibration parameters which engineers traditionally design by hand. This is taking place in an environment of rising customer expectations and steadily shorter product development cycles. At the same time, legislative requirements are increasing while emission standards are getting stricter. Considering the number of vehicle variants on top of all that, the conventional method is losing its practical and financial viability. Prior work has already demonstrated that optimal control functions can be automatically developed with reinforcement learning (RL); since the resulting functions are represented by artificial neural networks, they lack explainability, a circumstance which renders them challenging to employ in production vehicles. In this article, we present an explainable approach to automating the calibration process using residual RL which follows established automotive development principles. Its applicability is demonstrated by means of a map-based air path controller in a series control unit using a hardware-in-the-loop (HiL) platform. Starting with a sub-optimal map, the proposed methodology quickly converges to a calibration which closely resembles the reference in the series ECU. The results prove that the approach is suitable for the industry where it leads to better calibrations in significantly less time and requires virtually no human intervention

</details>


### [334] [Epistemic Robust Offline Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07072)
*Abhilash Reddy Chenreddy,Erick Delage*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper addresses challenges in offline reinforcement learning due to epistemic uncertainty, proposing a method based on compact uncertainty sets rather than ensembles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to improve robustness and generalization in offline reinforcement learning, particularly when data coverage is limited or biased due to behavior policies avoiding certain actions.

Method: The paper introduces a unified framework utilizing compact uncertainty sets for Q-values and an Epinet-based model to optimize cumulative reward without relying on ensembles.

Result: The proposed method demonstrates better robustness and generalization compared to ensemble-based baselines in both tabular and continuous domains.

Conclusion: The approach provides a more efficient and reliable solution to address epistemic uncertainty in offline RL, overcoming limitations of ensemble methods.

Abstract: Offline reinforcement learning learns policies from fixed datasets without further environment interaction. A key challenge in this setting is epistemic uncertainty, arising from limited or biased data coverage, particularly when the behavior policy systematically avoids certain actions. This can lead to inaccurate value estimates and unreliable generalization. Ensemble-based methods like SAC-N mitigate this by conservatively estimating Q-values using the ensemble minimum, but they require large ensembles and often conflate epistemic with aleatoric uncertainty. To address these limitations, we propose a unified and generalizable framework that replaces discrete ensembles with compact uncertainty sets over Q-values. %We further introduce an Epinet based model that directly shapes the uncertainty sets to optimize the cumulative reward under the robust Bellman objective without relying on ensembles. We also introduce a benchmark for evaluating offline RL algorithms under risk-sensitive behavior policies, and demonstrate that our method achieves improved robustness and generalization over ensemble-based baselines across both tabular and continuous state domains.

</details>


### [335] [Mining Electronic Health Records to Investigate Effectiveness of Ensemble Deep Clustering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07085)
*Manar D. Samad,Yina Hou,Shrabani Ghosh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper examines clustering methods for patient data in EHRs, introducing an ensemble-based deep clustering approach that enhances performance by combining traditional and deep learning methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve patient clustering and disease subtyping accuracy in EHR data, which are pivotal for understanding pathophysiology and supporting clinical decisions, amidst the limitations of current methods.

Method: The study compares traditional clustering, hybrid methods using autoencoders, and deep learning, proposing an ensemble-based deep clustering approach that aggregates results across multiple embedding dimensions and integrates traditional methods for enhanced accuracy.

Result: The novel ensemble embedding for deep clustering demonstrated the best performance across 14 clustering methods and various patient cohorts, offering significant advantages over singular approaches.

Conclusion: Combining traditional and deep clustering methods in the ensemble framework provides superior results, emphasizing the importance of tailoring approaches to EHR data and the need for biological sex-specific clustering.

Abstract: In electronic health records (EHRs), clustering patients and distinguishing disease subtypes are key tasks to elucidate pathophysiology and aid clinical decision-making. However, clustering in healthcare informatics is still based on traditional methods, especially K-means, and has achieved limited success when applied to embedding representations learned by autoencoders as hybrid methods. This paper investigates the effectiveness of traditional, hybrid, and deep learning methods in heart failure patient cohorts using real EHR data from the All of Us Research Program. Traditional clustering methods perform robustly because deep learning approaches are specifically designed for image clustering, a task that differs substantially from the tabular EHR data setting. To address the shortcomings of deep clustering, we introduce an ensemble-based deep clustering approach that aggregates cluster assignments obtained from multiple embedding dimensions, rather than relying on a single fixed embedding space. When combined with traditional clustering in a novel ensemble framework, the proposed ensemble embedding for deep clustering delivers the best overall performance ranking across 14 diverse clustering methods and multiple patient cohorts. This paper underscores the importance of biological sex-specific clustering of EHR data and the advantages of combining traditional and deep clustering approaches over a single method.

</details>


### [336] [Selective Neuron Amplification for Training-Free Task Enhancement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07098)
*Ryyan Akhtar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Selective Neuron Amplification (SNA), a method to enhance task-specific neuron activation in large language models during inference to mitigate performance failures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address a common problem where large language models fail on tasks they theoretically understand, highlighting weak neuronal activation as a potential cause.

Method: Selective Neuron Amplification (SNA) enhances task-relevant neurons' influence during inference without altering the model's parameters permanently.

Result: SNA is most effective in situations where the model shows uncertainty and has minimal impact when the model is already confident.

Conclusion: Model failures may stem from weak activation of relevant neurons rather than lacking capabilities, and SNA shows potential in addressing these issues.

Abstract: Large language models often fail on tasks they seem to already understand. In our experiments, this appears to be less about missing knowledge and more about certain internal circuits not being strongly activated during inference. We explore Selective Neuron Amplification, which increases the influence of task relevant neurons without changing the model's parameters. The method works at inference time and does not permanently alter the model. SNA helps mainly when the model is uncertain, while having low effect when the model is already confident. This suggests that some model failures are due to weak activation rather than lack of capability.

</details>


### [337] [Information as Structural Alignment: A Dynamical Theory of Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07108)
*Radu Negulescu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Informational Buildup Framework (IBF) as a new method for continual learning, emphasizing structural alignment rather than parameter superposition, achieving superior retention without storing raw data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for mitigating catastrophic forgetting rely heavily on supplementary mechanisms rather than addressing the issue through inherent learning dynamics.

Method: The authors propose IBF, governed by two equations—Law of Motion for coherence and Modification Dynamics for localized adjustments—tested through toy models, controlled environments, chess, and Split-CIFAR-100.

Result: IBF demonstrates near-zero forgetting in CIFAR-100, significant backward transfer in chess, and reduced forgetting compared to replay (43% improvement) in controlled tasks.

Conclusion: Informational Buildup Framework offers a promising substrate for continual learning, outperforming existing replay methods and showcasing memory retention as an emergent property.

Abstract: Catastrophic forgetting is not an engineering failure. It is a mathematical consequence of storing knowledge as global parameter superposition. Existing methods, such as regularization, replay, and frozen subnetworks, add external mechanisms to a shared-parameter substrate. None derives retention from the learning dynamics themselves.
  This paper introduces the Informational Buildup Framework (IBF), an alternative substrate for continual learning, based on the premise that information is the achievement of structural alignment rather than stored content. In IBF, two equations govern the dynamics: a Law of Motion that drives configuration toward higher coherence, and Modification Dynamics that persistently deform the coherence landscape in response to localized discrepancies. Memory, agency, and self-correction arise from these dynamics rather than being added as separate modules.
  We first demonstrate the full lifecycle in a transparent two-dimensional toy model, then validate across three domains: a controlled non-stationary world, chess evaluated independently by Stockfish, and Split-CIFAR-100 with a frozen ViT encoder. Across all three, IBF achieves replay-superior retention without storing raw data. We observe near-zero forgetting on CIFAR-100 (BT = -0.004), positive backward transfer in chess (+38.5 cp), and 43% less forgetting than replay in the controlled domain. In chess, the framework achieves a mean behavioral advantage of +88.9 +/- 2.8 cp under independent evaluation, exceeding MLP and replay baselines.

</details>


### [338] [Multi-Turn Reasoning LLMs for Task Offloading in Mobile Edge Computing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07148)
*Ning Yang,Chuangxin Cheng,Haijun Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents COMLLM, a generative framework for Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) that employs foresighted decision-making, achieves low latency, and generalizes to new network topologies without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mobile devices, constrained by limited resources, struggle to meet low-latency demands of computation-intensive applications. Existing methods like DRL and traditional heuristics are ineffective due to adaptability and generalization limits.

Method: The proposed method, COMLLM, integrates Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a Look-Ahead Collaborative Simulation (LACS) mechanism. Using multi-step Monte Carlo rollouts and server queue modeling, it enables long-term decision-making.

Result: Experimental results show COMLLM achieves near-optimal latency, improved load-balancing fairness, and generalizes to unseen large-scale networks without retraining, outperforming state-of-the-art approaches.

Conclusion: COMLLM establishes a robust, scalable, and efficient method for task offloading in MEC systems, solving key limitations of adaptability and foresight in existing frameworks.

Abstract: Emerging computation-intensive applications impose stringent latency requirements on resource-constrained mobile devices. Mobile Edge Computing (MEC) addresses this challenge through task offloading. However, designing effective policies remains difficult due to dynamic task arrivals, time-varying channels, and the spatio-temporal coupling of server queues. Conventional heuristics lack adaptability, while Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) suffers from limited generalization and architectural rigidity, requiring retraining when network topology changes. Although Large Language Models (LLMs) offer semantic reasoning capabilities, standard Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) yields myopic policies that greedily minimize immediate latency without accounting for long-term system evolution. To address these limitations, we propose COMLLM, a generative framework that enables foresighted decision-making in MEC systems. COMLLM integrates Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a Look-Ahead Collaborative Simulation (LACS) mechanism, which performs multi-step Monte Carlo rollouts while jointly modeling server queue dynamics. By incorporating these rollouts into the reward design, the framework captures the long-term impact of current decisions on future system states. Experimental results demonstrate that COMLLM achieves near-optimal latency and improved load-balancing fairness. Notably, it exhibits zero-shot topological scalability, allowing a model trained on small-scale networks to generalize to larger, unseen topologies without retraining, outperforming SFT, DRL, and heuristic baselines.

</details>


### [339] [SBBTS: A Unified Schrödinger-Bass Framework for Synthetic Financial Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07159)
*Alexandre Alouadi,Grégoire Loeper,Célian Marsala,Othmane Mazhar,Huyên Pham*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Schrödinger-Bass Bridge for Time Series (SBBTS), a framework for generating synthetic time series that replicates both drift and stochastic volatility, improving data augmentation and forecasting in financial machine learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper tackles the challenge of realistic synthetic time series generation in financial machine learning, addressing limitations in existing methods such as ignoring drift or fixing volatility, which restrict accurate modeling.

Method: The proposed method, SBBTS, extends Schrödinger-Bass theory to multi-step time series calibration. It employs diffusion processes and conditional transport problems for efficient modeling of drift and volatility.

Result: SBBTS successfully recovers stochastic volatility and correlation in numerical experiments, outperforms prior methods, and enhances forecasting accuracy and Sharpe ratio in S&P 500 data applications.

Conclusion: SBBTS offers a novel and effective framework for generating realistic time series, addressing key limitations in financial machine learning and improving downstream tasks such as forecasting and decision-making.

Abstract: We study the problem of generating synthetic time series that reproduce both marginal distributions and temporal dynamics, a central challenge in financial machine learning. Existing approaches typically fail to jointly model drift and stochastic volatility, as diffusion-based methods fix the volatility while martingale transport models ignore drift. We introduce the Schrödinger-Bass Bridge for Time Series (SBBTS), a unified framework that extends the Schrödinger-Bass formulation to multi-step time series. The method constructs a diffusion process that jointly calibrates drift and volatility and admits a tractable decomposition into conditional transport problems, enabling efficient learning. Numerical experiments on the Heston model demonstrate that SBBTS accurately recovers stochastic volatility and correlation parameters that prior SchrödingerBridge methods fail to capture. Applied to S&P 500 data, SBBTS-generated synthetic time series consistently improve downstream forecasting performance when used for data augmentation, yielding higher classification accuracy and Sharpe ratio compared to real-data-only training. These results show that SBBTS provides a practical and effective framework for realistic time series generation and data augmentation in financial applications.

</details>


### [340] [Smart Commander: A Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning Framework for Fleet-Level PHM Decision Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07171)
*Yong Si,Mingfei Lu,Jing Li,Yang Hu,Guijiang Li,Yueheng Song,Zhaokui Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Smart Commander, a Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning framework to optimize aviation PHM decision-making, overcoming challenges like dimensionality and sparse feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Military aviation PHM decision-making faces challenges such as complex fleet operations, sparse feedback, and stochastic mission profiles.

Method: The authors developed Smart Commander, a two-tier HRL system with strategic and tactical commanders to handle fleet availability and maintenance logistics, employing reward shaping and planning-enhanced neural networks.

Result: Smart Commander outperformed traditional DRL and rule-based methods, demonstrating reduced training time, superior scalability, and better reliability in high-failure scenarios.

Conclusion: HRL frameworks like Smart Commander show promise for scalable and robust fleet management in military aviation PHM contexts.

Abstract: Decision-making in military aviation Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) faces significant challenges due to the "curse of dimensionality" in large-scale fleet operations, combined with sparse feedback and stochastic mission profiles. To address these issues, this paper proposes Smart Commander, a novel Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning (HRL) framework designed to optimize sequential maintenance and logistics decisions. The framework decomposes the complex control problem into a two-tier hierarchy: a strategic General Commander manages fleet-level availability and cost objectives, while tactical Operation Commanders execute specific actions for sortie generation, maintenance scheduling, and resource allocation. The proposed approach is validated within a custom-built, high-fidelity discrete-event simulation environment that captures the dynamics of aircraft configuration and support logistics.By integrating layered reward shaping with planning-enhanced neural networks, the method effectively addresses the difficulty of sparse and delayed rewards. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that Smart Commander significantly outperforms conventional monolithic Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) and rule-based baselines. Notably, it achieves a substantial reduction in training time while demonstrating superior scalability and robustness in failure-prone environments. These results highlight the potential of HRL as a reliable paradigm for next-generation intelligent fleet management.

</details>


### [341] [Improving Semantic Uncertainty Quantification in Language Model Question-Answering via Token-Level Temperature Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07172)
*Tom A. Lamb,Desi R. Ivanova,Philip H. S. Torr,Tim G. J. Rudner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper highlights the importance of calibration in semantic uncertainty quantification, contrasting it with discrimination, and proposes temperature scaling as an effective method for improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior approaches to semantic uncertainty quantification have focused mainly on discrimination, neglecting calibration, resulting in incomplete reliability in confidence measures.

Method: The authors systematically evaluate confidence measures and introduce a scalar temperature optimization method as a straightforward way to improve calibration and discrimination.

Result: Temperature scaling enhances semantic calibration and discrimination in confidence measures, outperforming heuristic methods and token-level recalibration techniques, especially in question-answering tasks.

Conclusion: Optimizing a single scalar temperature is a simple and effective solution for improving both calibration and discrimination in semantic uncertainty quantification, offering a reliable and efficient approach over traditional methods.

Abstract: Calibration is central to reliable semantic uncertainty quantification, yet prior work has largely focused on discrimination, neglecting calibration. As calibration and discrimination capture distinct aspects of uncertainty, focusing on discrimination alone yields an incomplete picture. We address this gap by systematically evaluating both aspects across a broad set of confidence measures. We show that current approaches, particularly fixed-temperature heuristics, produce systematically miscalibrated and poorly discriminative semantic confidence distributions. We demonstrate that optimising a single scalar temperature, which, we argue, provides a suitable inductive bias, is a surprisingly simple yet effective solution. Our exhaustive evaluation confirms that temperature scaling consistently improves semantic calibration, discrimination, and downstream entropy, outperforming both heuristic baselines and more expressive token-level recalibration methods on question-answering tasks.

</details>


### [342] [Mixture Proportion Estimation and Weakly-supervised Kernel Test for Conditional Independence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07191)
*Yushi Hirose,Akito Narahara,Takafumi Kanamori*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a new approach to mixture proportion estimation (MPE) using conditional independence assumptions, which addresses limitations of prior methods relying on irreducibility assumptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MPE methods depend heavily on irreducibility assumptions that may not hold in all scenarios, limiting their applicability in weakly supervised learning tasks.

Method: The authors propose new conditional independence (CI) assumptions to ensure identifiability in MPE tasks, develop method of moments estimators based on these assumptions, and introduce kernel-based tests to validate the CI assumptions.

Result: Their estimators outperform existing MPE methods, and the proposed tests successfully control for type I and type II errors.

Conclusion: The conditional independence-based approach extends the applicability of MPE methods, providing robust tools for weakly supervised learning and related applications such as causal discovery and fairness evaluation.

Abstract: Mixture proportion estimation (MPE) aims to estimate class priors from unlabeled data. This task is a critical component in weakly supervised learning, such as PU learning, learning with label noise, and domain adaptation. Existing MPE methods rely on the \textit{irreducibility} assumption or its variant for identifiability. In this paper, we propose novel assumptions based on conditional independence (CI) given the class label, which ensure identifiability even when irreducibility does not hold. We develop method of moments estimators under these assumptions and analyze their asymptotic properties. Furthermore, we present weakly-supervised kernel tests to validate the CI assumptions, which are of independent interest in applications such as causal discovery and fairness evaluation. Empirically, we demonstrate the improved performance of our estimators compared with existing methods and that our tests successfully control both type I and type II errors.\label{key}

</details>


### [343] [Beyond the Mean: Modelling Annotation Distributions in Continuous Affect Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07198)
*Kosmas Pinitas,Ilias Maglogiannis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a distribution-aware approach using the Beta distribution for emotion annotation to capture variability and uncertainty in annotator perceptions, providing richer insights than conventional point-estimate methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods in continuous affect prediction reduce diverse and subjective emotional annotations into single point estimates (e.g., mean or median), which discard valuable information about annotator variability and uncertainty.

Method: The proposed framework predicts the mean and standard deviation of emotion annotations, then converts them into Beta distribution parameters using moment matching. This enables recovery of additional distributional descriptors like skewness and kurtosis.

Result: Experimental evaluations on SEWA and RECOLA datasets show that the Beta-based modelling aligns closely with empirical annotator distribution patterns and performs competitively against traditional regression models.

Conclusion: Modelling annotation uncertainty improves emotional perception understanding in affective computing, and distribution-aware approaches hold promise for analyzing subjective signals more comprehensively.

Abstract: Emotion annotation is inherently subjective and cognitively demanding, producing signals that reflect diverse perceptions across annotators rather than a single ground truth. In continuous affect prediction, this variability is typically collapsed into point estimates such as the mean or median, discarding valuable information about annotator disagreement and uncertainty. In this work, we propose a distribution-aware framework that models annotation consensus using the Beta distribution. Instead of predicting a single affect value, models estimate the mean and standard deviation of the annotation distribution, which are transformed into valid Beta parameters through moment matching. This formulation enables the recovery of higher-order distributional descriptors, including skewness, kurtosis, and quantiles, in closed form. As a result, the model captures not only the central tendency of emotional perception but also variability, asymmetry, and uncertainty in annotator responses. We evaluate the proposed approach on the SEWA and RECOLA datasets using multimodal features. Experimental results show that Beta-based modelling produces predictive distributions that closely match the empirical annotator distributions while achieving competitive performance with conventional regression approaches. These findings highlight the importance of modelling annotation uncertainty in affective computing and demonstrate the potential of distribution-aware learning for subjective signal analysis.

</details>


### [344] [Diffusion Processes on Implicit Manifolds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07213)
*Victor Kawasaki-Borruat,Clara Grotehans,Pierre Vandergheynst,Adam Gosztolai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method to construct diffusion processes on low-dimensional data manifolds using implicit settings, solely relying on point cloud samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aimed at addressing the challenge of constructing diffusion dynamics on low-dimensional data manifolds when geometric primitives like charts or projections aren't accessible.

Method: Develops a data-driven stochastic differential equation (SDE) by estimating the generator and carré-du-champ (CDC) of diffusion processes using proximity graphs derived from data.

Result: Demonstrates that the proposed process converges to the diffusion dynamics on smooth manifolds as sample numbers grow, and provides numerical simulation leveraging Euler-Maruyama integration.

Conclusion: Proposes the concept of Implicit Manifold-valued Diffusions (IMDs), enabling manifold-aware operations like sampling and generative modeling on data manifolds.

Abstract: High-dimensional data are often modeled as lying near a low-dimensional manifold. We study how to construct diffusion processes on this data manifold in the implicit setting. That is, using only point cloud samples and without access to charts, projections, or other geometric primitives. Our main contribution is a data-driven SDE that captures intrinsic diffusion on the underlying manifold while being defined in ambient space. The construction relies on estimating the diffusion's infinitesimal generator and its carré-du-champ (CDC) from a proximity graph built from the data. The generator and CDC together encode the local stochastic and geometric structure of the intended diffusion. We show that, as the number of samples grows, the induced process converges in law on the space of probability paths to its smooth manifold counterpart. We call this construction Implicit Manifold-valued Diffusions (IMDs), and furthermore present a numerical simulation procedure using Euler-Maruyama integration. This gives a rigorous basis for practical implementations of diffusion dynamics on data manifolds, and opens new directions for manifold-aware sampling, exploration, and generative modeling.

</details>


### [345] [How Does Machine Learning Manage Complexity?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07233)
*Lance Fortnow*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper explores the relationship between computational complexity and machine learning's ability to model complex systems, focusing on computable distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To better understand and manage the complexity in machine learning models, particularly regarding how they handle distributions beyond standard computable ones.

Method: The paper models machine learning as producing P/poly-computable distributions with polynomially-bounded max-entropy and analyzes error minimization against pseudorandom generator distributions.

Result: The study finds that when machine learning minimizes error against pseudorandom generator distributions, the resulting model's distribution is close to uniform.

Conclusion: Machine learning models can effectively manage complex systems by focusing on computable distributions, allowing better control over complexity via probability.

Abstract: We provide a computational complexity lens to understand the power of machine learning models, particularly their ability to model complex systems.
  Machine learning models are often trained on data drawn from sampleable or more complex distributions, a far wider range of distributions than just computable ones. By focusing on computable distributions, machine learning models can better manage complexity via probability. We abstract away from specific learning mechanisms, modeling machine learning as producing P/poly-computable distributions with polynomially-bounded max-entropy.
  We illustrate how learning computable distributions models complexity by showing that if a machine learning model produces a distribution $μ$ that minimizes error against the distribution generated by a cryptographic pseudorandom generator, then $μ$ must be close to uniform.

</details>


### [346] [On the Price of Privacy for Language Identification and Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07238)
*Xiaoyu Li,Andi Han,Jiaojiao Jiang,Junbin Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper examines the cost of maintaining differential privacy (DP) in language model tasks like identification and generation, quantifying the trade-off and showing minimal degradation in error rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address increasing privacy concerns as large language models (LLMs) are trained on sensitive user data, this paper explores the fundamental trade-off between privacy guarantees and language learning performance.

Method: The authors examine differential privacy in language identification and generation tasks, providing algorithmic frameworks and theoretical bounds for both approximate (ε, δ)-DP and pure ε-DP settings. They compare private and non-private error rates.

Result: Their findings reveal that for approximate DP, the non-private rates are preserved, while under pure DP, the cost of privacy introduces a degradation by a multiplicative factor of min{1, ε}, with tight upper and lower bounds for generation tasks.

Conclusion: The study concludes that the cost of privacy in language learning is relatively mild. Approximate DP imposes no additional error, while under pure DP, the penalties are predictably bounded, making the outcomes promising for private LLM training.

Abstract: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly trained on sensitive user data, understanding the fundamental cost of privacy in language learning becomes essential. We initiate the study of differentially private (DP) language identification and generation in the agnostic statistical setting, establishing algorithms and matching lower bounds that precisely quantify the cost of privacy. For both tasks, approximate $(\varepsilon, δ)$-DP with constant $\varepsilon > 0$ recovers the non-private error rates: $\exp(-r(n))$ for identification (for any $r(n) = o(n)$) and $\exp(-Ω(n))$ for generation. Under pure $\varepsilon$-DP, the exponents degrade by a multiplicative factor of $\min\{1, \varepsilon\}$, which we show is tight up to constants. Notably, for generation under pure DP with mild assumptions, the upper bound $\exp(-\min\{1,\varepsilon\} \cdot Ω(n))$ matches the lower bound up to some constants, establishing an optimal rate. Our results show that the cost of privacy in language learning is surprisingly mild: absent entirely under approximate DP, and exactly a $\min\{1,\varepsilon\}$ factor in the exponent under pure DP.

</details>


### [347] [Weaves, Wires, and Morphisms: Formalizing and Implementing the Algebra of Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07242)
*Vincent Abbott,Gioele Zardini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a categorical mathematical framework that formalizes deep learning architectures and their broadcasting properties, providing implementations in Python and TypeScript.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning lacks a formal mathematical framework to describe architectures, resulting in inconsistent representations and difficulty in managing nonlinear broadcasting.

Method: The authors propose a categorical framework using novel axis-stride and array-broadcasted categories, translating these math concepts into diagrams and machine-friendly data structures.

Result: The framework enables modular and precise representation of architectures and is implemented in Python and TypeScript for practical use, supporting algebraic construction, graph conversion, PyTorch compilation, and diagram rendering.

Conclusion: This work establishes a formal, systematic approach to designing and analyzing deep learning models, making the representation and manipulation of architectures more structured and consistent.

Abstract: Despite deep learning models running well-defined mathematical functions, we lack a formal mathematical framework for describing model architectures. Ad-hoc notation, diagrams, and pseudocode poorly handle nonlinear broadcasting and the relationship between individual components and composed models. This paper introduces a categorical framework for deep learning models that formalizes broadcasting through the novel axis-stride and array-broadcasted categories. This allows the mathematical function underlying architectures to be precisely expressed and manipulated in a compositional manner. These mathematical definitions are translated into human manageable diagrams and machine manageable data structures. We provide a mirrored implementation in Python (pyncd) and TypeScript (tsncd) to show the universal aspect of our framework, along with features including algebraic construction, graph conversion, PyTorch compilation and diagram rendering. This lays the foundation for a systematic, formal approach to deep learning model design and analysis.

</details>


### [348] [A comparative analysis of machine learning models in SHAP analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07258)
*Justin Lin,Julia Fukuyama*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper examines SHAP analysis for explaining complex machine learning models' predictions and proposes a new approach for multi-classification visualization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The increasing reliance on black-box AI models demands better interpretability due to their lack of transparency, especially in critical applications.

Method: This research investigates the application of SHAP analysis across various machine learning models and datasets and introduces a generalized waterfall plot for multi-class problems.

Result: The study provides detailed insights into SHAP analysis and proposes an enhanced visualization method for multi-class predictions.

Conclusion: Understanding SHAP values can demystify model decisions, leading to actionable insights, and the proposed visualization method advances interpretability in multi-class contexts.

Abstract: In this growing age of data and technology, large black-box models are becoming the norm due to their ability to handle vast amounts of data and learn incredibly complex data patterns. The deficiency of these methods, however, is their inability to explain the prediction process, making them untrustworthy and their use precarious in high-stakes situations. SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) analysis is an explainable AI method growing in popularity for its ability to explain model predictions in terms of the original features. For each sample and feature in the data set, an associated SHAP value quantifies the contribution of that feature to the prediction of that sample. Analysis of these SHAP values provides valuable insight into the model's decision-making process, which can be leveraged to create data-driven solutions. The interpretation of these SHAP values, however, is model-dependent, so there does not exist a universal analysis procedure. To aid in these efforts, we present a detailed investigation of SHAP analysis across various machine learning models and data sets. In uncovering the details and nuance behind SHAP analysis, we hope to empower analysts in this less-explored territory. We also present a novel generalization of the waterfall plot to the multi-classification problem.

</details>


### [349] [Tracking Adaptation Time: Metrics for Temporal Distribution Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07266)
*Lorenzo Iovine,Giacomo Ziffer,Emanuele Della Valle*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces three new metrics to evaluate how machine learning models adapt to temporal distribution shifts, revealing insights missed by existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current metrics for temporal robustness fail to distinguish between a model's adaptation capability and inherent data challenges, leading to misinterpretation of performance declines.

Method: Three complementary metrics are proposed to separate adaptation behavior from intrinsic data difficulty, providing a clearer view of model performance under temporal shifts.

Result: The proposed metrics reveal new adaptation patterns not captured by existing approaches, offering improved interpretability of model behavior in dynamic environments.

Conclusion: The new metrics allow for a deeper understanding of temporal robustness, helping disentangle model adaptation from data complexity and improving evaluation approaches in evolving scenarios.

Abstract: Evaluating robustness under temporal distribution shift remains an open challenge. Existing metrics quantify the average decline in performance, but fail to capture how models adapt to evolving data. As a result, temporal degradation is often misinterpreted: when accuracy declines, it is unclear whether the model is failing to adapt or whether the data itself has become inherently more challenging to learn. In this work, we propose three complementary metrics to distinguish adaptation from intrinsic difficulty in the data. Together, these metrics provide a dynamic and interpretable view of model behavior under temporal distribution shift. Results show that our metrics uncover adaptation patterns hidden by existing analysis, offering a richer understanding of temporal robustness in evolving environments.

</details>


### [350] [Android Coach: Improve Online Agentic Training Efficiency with Single State Multiple Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07277)
*Guo Gan,Yuxuan Ding,Cong Chen,Yuwei Ren,Yin Huang,Hong Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Android Coach, a novel RL framework improving Android agent training efficiency and effectiveness by shifting the training paradigm from Single State Single Action to Single State Multiple Actions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Guiding Android agents through online RL interactions is prohibitively expensive due to high emulator latencies and sample inefficiency of current algorithms.

Method: Android Coach uses a critic to estimate action values, enabling sampling multiple actions per online state without additional emulator overhead. It incorporates a reward model and group-wise advantage estimation based on averaged critic outputs.

Result: Android Coach achieved 7.5% and 8.3% success rate improvements over state-of-the-art systems in AndroidLab and AndroidWorld, and demonstrated 1.4x higher training efficiency than PPO and GRPO.

Conclusion: The framework significantly enhances RL efficiency and success rates for Android agents by optimizing state-action exploration within existing constraints.

Abstract: Online reinforcement learning (RL) serves as an effective method for enhancing the capabilities of Android agents. However, guiding agents to learn through online interaction is prohibitively expensive due to the high latency of emulators and the sample inefficiency of existing RL algorithms. We identify a fundamental limitation in current approaches: the Single State Single Action paradigm, which updates the policy with one-to-one state-action pairs from online one-way rollouts without fully exploring each costly emulator state. In this paper, we propose Android Coach, a novel framework that shifts the training paradigm to Single State Multiple Actions, allowing the agent to sample and utilize multiple actions for a single online state. We enable this without additional emulator overhead by learning a critic that estimates action values. To ensure the critic serves as a reliable coach, we integrate a process reward model and introduce a group-wise advantage estimator based on the averaged critic outputs. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of Android Coach: it achieves 7.5% and 8.3% success rate improvements on AndroidLab and AndroidWorld over UI-TARS-1.5-7B, and attains 1.4x higher training efficiency than Single State Single Action methods PPO and GRPO at matched success rates.

</details>


### [351] [Graph Neural ODE Digital Twins for Control-Oriented Reactor Thermal-Hydraulic Forecasting Under Partial Observability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07292)
*Akzhol Almukhametov,Doyeong Lim,Rui Hu,Yang Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces a physics-informed Graph Neural Network coupled with Neural Ordinary Differential Equation (GNN-ODE) for real-time thermal-hydraulic state prediction in advanced reactors, showcasing high predictive fidelity, fast inference, and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to enhance supervisory control in advanced reactors by developing surrogate models capable of accurate, quick, and robust thermal-hydraulic state predictions for locations lacking physical sensors.

Method: The method involves designing a GNN-ODE framework that uses sensor graph representations, message passing for hydraulic connectivity, and Neural ODE to model latent dynamics. Missing-node states are initialized with topology-guided methods, and predictions proceed autoregressively.

Result: The GNN-ODE achieves robust predictive performance with MAE scores of 0.91 K and 2.18 K at 60 s and 300 s, respectively, and $R^2$ up to 0.995 for missing-node reconstruction. Inference speed is significantly faster (105x simulated time), with effective transfer learning demonstrated using minimal experimental data.

Conclusion: The GNN-ODE surrogate is highly effective for real-time supervisory control, providing high-fidelity predictions, fast computational efficiency, and the ability to adapt to experimental data, making it suitable for advanced reactor applications.

Abstract: Real-time supervisory control of advanced reactors requires accurate forecasting of plant-wide thermal-hydraulic states, including locations where physical sensors are unavailable. Meeting this need calls for surrogate models that combine predictive fidelity, millisecond-scale inference, and robustness to partial observability. In this work, we present a physics-informed message-passing Graph Neural Network coupled with a Neural Ordinary Differential Equation (GNN-ODE) to addresses all three requirements simultaneously. We represent the whole system as a directed sensor graph whose edges encode hydraulic connectivity through flow/heat transfer-aware message passing, and we advance the latent dynamics in continuous time via a controlled Neural ODE. A topology-guided missing-node initializer reconstructs uninstrumented states at rollout start; prediction then proceeds fully autoregressively. The GNN-ODE surrogate achieves satisfactory results for the system dynamics prediction. On held-out simulation transients, the surrogate achieves an average MAE of 0.91 K at 60 s and 2.18 K at 300 s for uninstrumented nodes, with $R^2$ up to 0.995 for missing-node state reconstruction. Inference runs at approximately 105 times faster than simulated time on a single GPU, enabling 64-member ensemble rollouts for uncertainty quantification. To assess sim-to-real transfer, we adapt the pretrained surrogate to experimental facility data using layerwise discriminative fine-tuning with only 30 training sequences. The learned flow-dependent heat-transfer scaling recovers a Reynolds-number exponent consistent with established correlations, indicating constitutive learning beyond trajectory fitting. The model tracks a steep power change transient and produces accurate trajectories at uninstrumented locations.

</details>


### [352] [SL-FAC: A Communication-Efficient Split Learning Framework with Frequency-Aware Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07316)
*Zehang Lin,Miao Yang,Haihan Zhu,Zheng Lin,Jianhao Huang,Jing Yang,Guangjin Pan,Dianxin Luan,Zihan Fang,Shunzhi Zhu,Wei Ni,John Thompson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces SL-FAC, a communication-efficient Split Learning framework using frequency domain transformations and quantization to reduce communication overhead while maintaining model performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The high communication overhead from transmitting smashed data in Split Learning hinders its scalability in distributed machine learning on resource-constrained devices.

Method: SL-FAC utilizes adaptive frequency decomposition (AFD) to transform smashed data into the frequency domain and decompose it into spectral components, followed by frequency-based quantization compression (FQC) to apply optimized bit widths based on spectral energy.

Result: The proposed framework significantly reduces communication requirements while preserving the key information needed for model convergence.

Conclusion: SL-FAC effectively addresses the communication bottleneck in Split Learning, making distributed training more practical for resource-limited environments and demonstrating improved training efficiency in experiments.

Abstract: The growing complexity of neural networks hinders the deployment of distributed machine learning on resource-constrained devices. Split learning (SL) offers a promising solution by partitioning the large model and offloading the primary training workload from edge devices to an edge server. However, the increasing number of participating devices and model complexity leads to significant communication overhead from the transmission of smashed data (e.g., activations and gradients), which constitutes a critical bottleneck for SL. To tackle this challenge, we propose SL-FAC, a communication-efficient SL framework comprising two key components: adaptive frequency decomposition (AFD) and frequency-based quantization compression (FQC). AFD first transforms the smashed data into the frequency domain and decomposes it into spectral components with distinct information. FQC then applies customized quantization bit widths to each component based on its spectral energy distribution. This collaborative approach enables SL-FAC to achieve significant communication reduction while strategically preserving the information most crucial for model convergence. Extensive experiments confirm the superior performance of SL-FAC for improving the training efficiency.

</details>


### [353] [How to sketch a learning algorithm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07328)
*Sam Gunn*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a data deletion scheme that can predict how an AI model would behave if specific training data were excluded, with minimal error and efficient precomputation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding how training data influences AI models is critical for interpretability, privacy concerns, and foundational studies in AI.

Method: The researchers propose a data deletion scheme leveraging stability assumptions and forward-mode automatic differentiation to efficiently compute higher-order derivatives in random complex directions.

Result: The method can predict model outputs with negligible error ($\varepsilon$) and operates with computational and storage requirements only polynomially greater than standard training and inference. Experiments with microgpt demonstrate the validity of the approach.

Conclusion: The proposed approach provides a viable, efficient solution to the data deletion problem while maintaining compatibility with training powerful AI models.

Abstract: How does the choice of training data influence an AI model? This question is of central importance to interpretability, privacy, and basic science. At its core is the data deletion problem: after a reasonable amount of precomputation, quickly predict how the model would behave in a given situation if a given subset of training data had been excluded from the learning algorithm.
  We present a data deletion scheme capable of predicting model outputs with vanishing error $\varepsilon$ in the deep learning setting. Our precomputation and prediction algorithms are only $\mathrm{poly}(1/\varepsilon)$ factors slower than regular training and inference, respectively. The storage requirements are those of $\mathrm{poly}(1/\varepsilon)$ models.
  Our proof is based on an assumption that we call "stability." In contrast to the assumptions made by prior work, stability appears to be fully compatible with learning powerful AI models. In support of this, we show that stability is satisfied in a minimal set of experiments with microgpt. Our code is available at https://github.com/SamSpo1/microgpt-sketch.
  At a technical level, our work is based on a new method for locally sketching an arithmetic circuit by computing higher-order derivatives in random complex directions. Forward-mode automatic differentiation allows cheap computation of these derivatives.

</details>


<div id='cs.NE'></div>

# cs.NE [[Back]](#toc)

### [354] [The Traveling Thief Problem with Time Windows: Benchmarks and Heuristics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06724)
*Helen Yuliana Angmalisang,Frank Neumann*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The study introduces a new variant of the traveling thief problem (TTP) by incorporating time window constraints, proposes a new heuristic approach for this variant, and demonstrates its superior performance on newly designed benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The research is motivated by the need to address real-world optimization problems that involve interdependencies between components, specifically in cases where goods can only be collected within specified time intervals.

Method: The paper proposes adaptions of existing TTP and TSP approaches to solve the TTP with time window constraints and introduces a new heuristic algorithm tailored for this problem. It also designs new benchmark instances to evaluate the algorithms.

Result: The new heuristic algorithm for TTP with time window constraints outperforms other adapted approaches when tested on a comprehensive set of newly introduced benchmark instances.

Conclusion: The study successfully extends TTP by adding time window constraints and provides an effective heuristic solution that demonstrates superior performance in solving this enriched optimization problem.

Abstract: While traditional optimization problems were often studied in isolation, many real-world problems today require interdependence among multiple optimization components. The traveling thief problem (TTP) is a multi-component problem that has been widely studied in the literature. In this paper, we introduce and investigate the TTP with time window constraints which provides a TTP variant highly relevant to real-world situations where good can only be collected at given time intervals. We examine adaptions of existing approaches for TTP and the Traveling Salesperson Problem (TSP) with time windows to this new problem and evaluate their performance. Furthermore, we provide a new heuristic approach for the TTP with time windows. To evaluate algorithms for TTP with time windows, we introduce new TTP benchmark instances with time windows based on TTP instances existing in the literature. Our experimental investigations evaluate the different approaches and show that the newly designed algorithm outperforms the other approaches on a wide range of benchmark instances.

</details>


### [355] [Block-Bench: A Framework for Controllable and Transparent Discrete Optimization Benchmarking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06973)
*Furong Ye,Frank Neumann,Thomas Bäck,Niki van Stein*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method to design discrete optimization benchmarks with controllable properties for analyzing algorithm behavior in detail, particularly for large-scale problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for benchmarks that facilitate a deeper understanding of algorithm behaviors, especially in complex and multi-modal discrete optimization problems.

Method: Benchmarks are built using block functions, weight factors, and adjacency graphs, allowing control over problem properties and providing insights into both objective space and variable representation analyses.

Result: The framework enables detailed analysis of heuristic behaviors in large-scale problems and inspires advancements in self-adaptation, diversity control, and other algorithmic domains.

Conclusion: The proposed benchmark design enhances research potential across diverse areas, including dynamic algorithm configuration and multi-objective optimization, and provides new ways to study algorithm performance in controlled scenarios.

Abstract: We present a novel approach for constructing discrete optimization benchmarks that enables fine-grained control over problem properties, and such benchmarks can facilitate analyzing discrete algorithm behaviors. We build benchmark problems based on a set of block functions, where each block function maps a subset of variables to a real value. Problems are instantiated through a set of block functions, weight factors, and an adjacency graph representing the dependency among the block functions. Through analyzing intermediate block values, our framework allows to analyze algorithm behavior not only in the objective space but also at the level of variable representations in the obtained solutions. This capacity is particularly useful for analyzing discrete heuristics in large-scale multi-modal problems, thereby enhancing the practical relevance of benchmark studies. We demonstrate how the proposed approach can inspire the related work in self-adaptation and diversity control in evolutionary algorithms. Moreover, we explain that the proposed benchmark design enables explicit control over problem properties, supporting research in broader domains such as dynamic algorithm configuration and multi-objective optimization.

</details>


### [356] [Anytime Analysis on BinVal: Adaptive Parameters Help](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06976)
*Timo Kötzing,Jurek Sander*

Main category: cs.NE

TL;DR: The paper examines fixed-target run times for evolutionary and estimation-of-distribution algorithms on a specific fitness function, providing bounds that hold simultaneously for all $k \in o(n)$. Results showcase varying efficiencies depending on algorithm choices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The main motivation is to focus on the anytime performance of search heuristics over fixed-time analyses, allowing insights into optimization progress across different target levels.

Method: The paper analyzes fixed-target run times using theoretical analyses across different algorithms applied to the BinVal fitness function, considering both standard and modified mutation rates.

Result: Key findings include fixed-target bounds on run times: $Θ(n \log k)$ for (1+1) EA with fixed mutation rate, $Θ(k \log n)$ for sig-cGA, and $\mathcal{O}\left(k^{1+\varepsilon}\right)$ for self-adjusting mutation rate in (1+1) EA.

Conclusion: Results demonstrate that algorithm variants can significantly influence run times, with self-adjusting mutation rates achieving near-optimal performance while being independent of problem size $n$.

Abstract: While most theoretical run time analyses of discrete randomized search heuristics provide bounds on the expected number of evaluations to find the global optimum, we consider the anytime performance of evolutionary and estimation-of-distribution algorithms. For this purpose, we analyze the fixed-target run time of various algorithms using BinVal as fitness function and bound the run time to optimize the most significant $k \in o(n)$ bits of a bit string with length $n$. We analyze the run times such that they hold not only for a fixed $k$, but simultaneously for all $k \in o(n)$.
  For the standard (1+1) EA with fixed mutation rate $1/n$, we show that the fixed-target run time for all $k \in o(n)$ is in $Θ(n \log k)$. Using an EDA instead, we get an expected number of evaluations of $Θ(k \log n)$ for the sig-cGA. Replacing in the standard (1+1) EA the fixed mutation rate with a self-adjusting rate, we show that the fixed-target run time for $k \in o(n)$ and a constant $\varepsilon >0$ arbitrarily close to zero is in $\mathcal{O}\left(k^{1+\varepsilon}\right)$ for this algorithm. In particular, this run time is independent of $n$, holds simultaneously for all $k \in o(n)$, and is close to the run time of $Θ(k \log k)$ for the (1+1) EA with the best fixed mutation rate if $k$ is known.

</details>


<div id='cs.PL'></div>

# cs.PL [[Back]](#toc)

### [357] [Parametrizing Reads-From Equivalence for Predictive Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06533)
*Azadeh Farzan,Umang Mathur*

Main category: cs.PL

TL;DR: This paper introduces $k$-sliced reorderings to balance predictive runtime monitoring's efficacy and efficiency by controlling the level of execution reorderings while maintaining a constant-space streaming algorithm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods in predictive runtime monitoring either lack computational feasibility or predictive power due to the trade-off between complexity of properties and expressive power of reorderings. The paper aims to address this limitation through a parameterized approach.

Method: The authors propose $k$-sliced reorderings as a new methodology to allow a controlled hierarchy of execution reorderings, ensuring program order and reads-from constraints are preserved. They also develop a constant-space streaming algorithm tailored for this framework.

Result: $k$-sliced reorderings provide a strictly increasing hierarchy that balances between computational efficiency and expressiveness, enabling predictive monitoring for any regular specification using constant space.

Conclusion: This paper establishes $k$-sliced reorderings as a robust framework for predictive runtime monitoring, offering an adjustable trade-off between computational cost and expressive power while maintaining efficiency through constant-space algorithms.

Abstract: Predictive runtime monitoring asks whether an execution $σ$ of a concurrent program can be used to \emph{soundly predict} the existence of a reordering $ρ$ of $σ$ that satisfies a property $\varphi$.
  Its effectiveness and efficiency depend on two factors: (a) the complexity of $\varphi$, and (b) the expressive power of the reorderings considered. At one extreme, allowing all reorderings induced by \emph{reads-from equivalence} makes predictive monitoring intractable, even for simple properties such as data races. At the other extreme, restricting to commutativity-based reorderings (Mazurkiewicz trace equivalence) yields efficient algorithms for simple properties, but remains intractable for general regular specifications and offers limited predictive power.
  We address this tradeoff via \emph{parametrization}. We introduce \emph{sliced reorderings} and their generalization, \emph{$k$-sliced reorderings}. Informally, $ρ$ is a $k$-sliced reordering of $σ$ if $σ$ can be partitioned into $k+1$ ordered subsequences whose concatenation yields $ρ$, while preserving program order and reads-from constraints.
  Our results are twofold. First, $k$-sliced reorderings form a strictly increasing hierarchy that converges to reads-from equivalence as $k$ grows. Second, for any fixed $k$, predictive monitoring modulo $k$-sliced reorderings against any regular specification admits a constant-space streaming algorithm.
  Together, these results establish $k$-sliced reorderings as a principled alternative to existing equivalences, enabling a uniform parametrized framework where expressive power can be systematically traded off against computational cost.

</details>


### [358] [Modelling Distributed Applications with Mixed-Choice Stateful Typestates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06874)
*Francisco Parrinha,João Mota,António Ravara*

Main category: cs.PL
