Blockchain provides decentralized, immutable, and transparent mechanisms that strengthen data integrity, trust, and accountability. In parallel, AI offers predictive analytics, anomaly detection, and adaptive defense capabilities to enable proactive threat identification and mitigation. The chapter discusses how Blockchain supports security in cyber-physical systems, how AI enables proactive security operations, and how their combination creates robust, adaptive, and trustworthy security frameworks. The chapter also explores the emerging role of large language models in threat intelligence and analyzes how controlled agentic AI can support bounded security workflows such as alert triage, evidence collection, and policy-aware response planning. Representative case studies illustrate the potential of these technologies to enhance cyber resilience. Finally, challenges related to scalability, energy efficiency, and ethical considerations are addressed, along with reported mitigation strategies and future research directions. Overall, this chapter provides researchers, practitioners, and policymakers with insights to design secure, resilient, and adaptable intelligent networks.

</details>


### [447] [Adversarial Robustness of Time-Series Classification for Crystal Collimator Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06289)
*Xaver Fink,Borja Fernandez Adiego,Daniele Mirarchi,Eloise Matheson,Alvaro Garcia Gonzales,Gianmarco Ricci,Joost-Pieter Katoen*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper tackles the adversarial robustness of a convolutional neural network (CNN) used for beam-loss monitoring at CERN's LHC and proposes preprocessing enhancements to improve robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the reliability of a CNN assisting crystal-collimator alignment at the LHC by making it less susceptible to adversarial attacks.

Method: The paper introduces preprocessing-aware wrappers and evaluates adversarial robustness using frameworks like Foolbox and ART, followed by adversarial fine-tuning of the CNN.

Result: Improved robust accuracy by up to 18.6% without degrading clean accuracy; extended robustness insights to sequence-level time-series data.

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates the importance of preprocessing for adversarial robustness and shows that such strategies can enhance performance without compromising normal operational metrics.

Abstract: In this paper, we analyze and improve the adversarial robustness of a convolutional neural network (CNN) that assists crystal-collimator alignment at CERN's Large Hadron Collider (LHC) by classifying a beam-loss monitor (BLM) time series during crystal rotation. We formalize a local robustness property for this classifier under an adversarial threat model based on real-world plausibility. Building on established parameterized input-transformation patterns used for transformation- and semantic-perturbation robustness, we instantiate a preprocessing-aware wrapper for our deployed time-series pipeline: we encode time-series normalization, padding constraints, and structured perturbations as a lightweight differentiable wrapper in front of the CNN, so that existing gradient-based robustness frameworks can operate on the deployed pipeline. For formal verification, data-dependent preprocessing such as per-window z-normalization introduces nonlinear operators that require verifier-specific abstractions. We therefore focus on attack-based robustness estimates and pipeline-checked validity by benchmarking robustness with the frameworks Foolbox and ART. Adversarial fine-tuning of the resulting CNN improves robust accuracy by up to 18.6 % without degrading clean accuracy. Finally, we extend robustness on time-series data beyond single windows to sequence-level robustness for sliding-window classification, introduce adversarial sequences as counterexamples to a temporal robustness requirement over full scans, and observe attack-induced misclassifications that persist across adjacent windows.

</details>


### [448] [FedSpy-LLM: Towards Scalable and Generalizable Data Reconstruction Attacks from Gradients on LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06297)
*Syed Irfan Ali Meerza,Feiyi Wang,Jian Liu*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces FedSpy-LLM, a data reconstruction attack targeting federated language models, addressing challenges when using PEFT and offering scalability and generalizability across architectures with improved reconstruction methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation arises from the need to address privacy risks in federated learning with private data for training large language models, particularly when parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods complicate the attack surface.

Method: FedSpy-LLM relies on innovative techniques including a gradient decomposition strategy and iterative gradient alignment to extract and order data efficiently, even under the constraints of PEFT and with diverse model architectures.

Result: The proposed method successfully reconstructs larger batch sizes, longer sequences, and diverse architectures—including encoder-decoder models—while overcoming challenges introduced by PEFT's null space.

Conclusion: FedSpy-LLM broadens the practical understanding of privacy vulnerabilities in federated learning, even when PEFT is used, uncovering risks that must be addressed to safeguard private data.

Abstract: Given the growing reliance on private data in training Large Language Models (LLMs), Federated Learning (FL) combined with Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) has garnered significant attention for enhancing privacy and efficiency. Despite FL's privacy benefits, prior studies have shown that private data can still be extracted from shared gradients. However, these studies, mainly on full-parameter model training, are limited to reconstructing small batches, short input sequences, and specific model architectures, such as encoder-based or decoder-based models. The reconstruction quality becomes even worse when dealing with gradients from PEFT methods. To fully understand the practical attack surface of federated LLMs, this paper proposes FedSpy-LLM, a scalable and generalizable data reconstruction attack designed to reconstruct training data with larger batch sizes and longer sequences while generalizing across diverse model architectures, even when PEFT methods are deployed for training. At the core of FedSpy-LLM is a novel gradient decomposition strategy that exploits the rank deficiency and subspace structure of gradients, enabling efficient token extraction while preserving key signal components at scale. This approach further mitigates the reconstruction challenges introduced by PEFT's substantial null space, ensuring robustness across encoder-based, decoder-based, and encoder-decoder model architectures. Additionally, by iteratively aligning each token's partial-sequence gradient with the full-sequence gradient, FedSpy-LLM ensures accurate token ordering in reconstructed sequences.

</details>


### [449] [WebSP-Eval: Evaluating Web Agents on Website Security and Privacy Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06367)
*Guruprasad Viswanathan Ramesh,Asmit Nayak,Basieem Siddique,Kassem Fawaz*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces WebSP-Eval, a framework for evaluating web agents' ability to execute user-facing website security and privacy tasks, analyzing 8 web agents using a custom task dataset, agentic system, and automation tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks fail to assess agent performance on user-facing website security and privacy tasks.

Method: The framework includes a manually crafted dataset of 200 tasks across 28 websites, an agentic system for state management, and an automated evaluator. 8 web agent models' performance is examined.

Result: Web agents struggle with autonomous exploration and performing security/privacy tasks, particularly with stateful UI elements like toggles and checkboxes, which fail 45% of the time.

Conclusion: Current models are inadequate for reliably solving security and privacy tasks due to challenges with specific task categories, websites, and UI elements, highlighting a need for improved exploration and UI handling capabilities.

Abstract: Web agents automate browser tasks, ranging from simple form completion to complex workflows like ordering groceries. While current benchmarks evaluate general-purpose performance~(e.g., WebArena) or safety against malicious actions~(e.g., SafeArena), no existing framework assesses an agent's ability to successfully execute user-facing website security and privacy tasks, such as managing cookie preferences, configuring privacy-sensitive account settings, or revoking inactive sessions. To address this gap, we introduce WebSP-Eval, an evaluation framework for measuring web agent performance on website security and privacy tasks. WebSP-Eval comprises 1) a manually crafted task dataset of 200 task instances across 28 websites; 2) a robust agentic system supporting account and initial state management across runs using a custom Google Chrome extension; and 3) an automated evaluator. We evaluate a total of 8 web agent instantiations using state-of-the-art multimodal large language models, conducting a fine-grained analysis across websites, task categories, and UI elements. Our evaluation reveals that current models suffer from limited autonomous exploration capabilities to reliably solve website security and privacy tasks, and struggle with specific task categories and websites. Crucially, we identify stateful UI elements such as toggles and checkboxes are a primary reason for agent failure, failing at a rate of more than 45\% in tasks containing these elements across many models.

</details>


### [450] [Towards Resilient Intrusion Detection in CubeSats: Challenges, TinyML Solutions, and Future Directions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06411)
*Yasamin Fayyaz,Li Yang,Khalil El-Khatib*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper discusses cybersecurity challenges for CubeSats, particularly due to their reliance on COTS components, and explores solutions like TinyML and anomaly detection techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CubeSats are increasingly vital in space missions, but their reliance on COTS components and open-source technologies makes them susceptible to cyber threats.

Method: The paper conducts an in-depth review of current cybersecurity practices, examines non-cyber anomaly detection techniques, and suggests integrating TinyML for intrusion detection.

Result: Open research problems such as resource-efficient IDS mechanisms and the need for cybersecurity frameworks are identified. TinyML is highlighted as a potential solution for real-time intrusion detection in CubeSats.

Conclusion: Cybersecurity solutions tailored to CubeSat constraints are essential. Collaborations between cybersecurity experts and space domain specialists, along with advancements like TinyML, can address these challenges effectively.

Abstract: CubeSats have revolutionized access to space by providing affordable and accessible platforms for research and education. However, their reliance on Commercial Off-The-Shelf (COTS) components and open-source software has introduced significant cybersecurity vulnerabilities. Ensuring the cybersecurity of CubeSats is vital as they play increasingly important roles in space missions. Traditional security measures, such as intrusion detection systems (IDS), are impractical for CubeSats due to resource constraints and unique operational environments. This paper provides an in-depth review of current cybersecurity practices for CubeSats, highlighting limitations and identifying gaps in existing methods. Additionally, it explores non-cyber anomaly detection techniques that offer insights into adaptable algorithms and deployment strategies suitable for CubeSat constraints. Open research problems are identified, including the need for resource-efficient intrusion detection mechanisms, evaluation of IDS solutions under realistic mission scenarios, development of autonomous response systems, and creation of cybersecurity frameworks. The addition of TinyML into CubeSat systems is explored as a promising solution to address these challenges, offering resource-efficient, real-time intrusion detection capabilities. Future research directions are proposed, such as integrating cybersecurity with health monitoring systems, and fostering collaboration between cybersecurity researchers and space domain experts.

</details>


### [451] [Say Something Else: Rethinking Contextual Privacy as Information Sufficiency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06409)
*Yunze Xiao,Wenkai Li,Xiaoyuan Wu,Ningshan Ma,Yueqi Song,Weihao Xuan*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper focuses on improving privacy in communications supported by LLMs through a new strategy called 'free-text pseudonymization' and evaluates its effectiveness in realistic conversational settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Users often overshare sensitive information when interacting with LLM agents, and existing systems fail to fully address privacy needs, leaving room for improvement in both strategies and evaluation methods.

Method: The paper introduces 'free-text pseudonymization' as a new privacy-preserving strategy, formalizes privacy as an Information Sufficiency (IS) task, and proposes a conversational evaluation protocol to assess strategies in multi-turn conversations.

Result: The study evaluates seven LLMs across diverse scenarios and finds that pseudonymization offers the best privacy-utility tradeoff. It also identifies significant shortcomings in single-message evaluations, which underestimate information leakage.

Conclusion: Free-text pseudonymization is a promising strategy for balancing privacy and utility in LLM communications, while single-message evaluations are insufficient for understanding total privacy risks.

Abstract: LLM agents increasingly draft messages on behalf of users, yet users routinely overshare sensitive information and disagree on what counts as private. Existing systems support only suppression (omitting sensitive information) and generalization (replacing information with an abstraction), and are typically evaluated on single isolated messages, leaving both the strategy space and evaluation setting incomplete. We formalize privacy-preserving LLM communication as an \textbf{Information Sufficiency (IS)} task, introduce \textbf{free-text pseudonymization} as a third strategy that replaces sensitive attributes with functionally equivalent alternatives, and propose a \textbf{conversational evaluation protocol} that assesses strategies under realistic multi-turn follow-up pressure. Across 792 scenarios spanning three power-relation types (institutional, peer, intimate) and three sensitivity categories (discrimination risk, social cost, boundary), we evaluate seven frontier LLMs on privacy at two granularities, covertness, and utility. Pseudonymization yields the strongest privacy\textendash utility tradeoff overall, and single-message evaluation systematically underestimates leakage, with generalization losing up to 16.3 percentage points of privacy under follow-up.

</details>


### [452] [The Defense Trilemma: Why Prompt Injection Defense Wrappers Fail?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06436)
*Manish Bhatt,Sarthak Munshi,Vineeth Sai Narajala,Idan Habler,Ammar Al-Kahfah,Ken Huang,Blake Gatto*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper demonstrates that continuous and utility-preserving preprocessor defenses for language models are inherently limited and characterizes where such methods fail, forming a trilemma between continuity, utility preservation, and completeness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the theoretical boundaries and limitations of preprocessors meant to safeguard outputs of language models, ensuring safe interaction without compromising utility.

Method: The paper provides a set of theoretical proofs under varying conditions (e.g., boundary fixation, $ε$-robust constraints) and empirically validates the findings on three large language models. The core theory is verified using Lean 4.

Result: The authors highlight a 'defense trilemma,' showing the impossibility of achieving continuity, utility preservation, and complete safety simultaneously in preprocessors. They also extend their theoretical findings to various practical scenarios.

Conclusion: No continuous, utility-preserving preprocessing defense can universally ensure safety in language models, but the results do not exclude alternative approaches like training-time alignment or sacrificing utility for safety.

Abstract: We prove that no continuous, utility-preserving wrapper defense-a function $D: X\to X$ that preprocesses inputs before the model sees them-can make all outputs strictly safe for a language model with connected prompt space, and we characterize exactly where every such defense must fail. We establish three results under successively stronger hypotheses: boundary fixation-the defense must leave some threshold-level inputs unchanged; an $ε$-robust constraint-under Lipschitz regularity, a positive-measure band around fixed boundary points remains near-threshold; and a persistent unsafe region under a transversality condition, a positive-measure subset of inputs remains strictly unsafe. These constitute a defense trilemma: continuity, utility preservation, and completeness cannot coexist. We prove parallel discrete results requiring no topology, and extend to multi-turn interactions, stochastic defenses, and capacity-parity settings. The results do not preclude training-time alignment, architectural changes, or defenses that sacrifice utility. The full theory is mechanically verified in Lean 4 and validated empirically on three LLMs.

</details>


### [453] [SkillSieve: A Hierarchical Triage Framework for Detecting Malicious AI Agent Skills](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06550)
*Yinghan Hou,Zongyou Yang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: SkillSieve is a detection framework addressing security vulnerabilities in OpenClaw's ClawHub marketplace, achieving high precision by combining regex, LLM analysis, and LLM jury evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the limitations of existing methods in detecting security vulnerabilities in ClawHub's agent skills, which are usually hidden in both code and natural language instructions.

Method: It introduces a three-layer framework: Layer 1 filters benign skills quickly using regex, AST, and metadata checks; Layer 2 performs focused LLM analysis across four sub-tasks; Layer 3 uses a jury of LLMs for final evaluation in highly suspicious cases.

Result: SkillSieve outperformed existing tools like ClawVet on accuracy, achieving 0.800 F1 on a labeled benchmark, while maintaining low average cost and computational efficiency.

Conclusion: SkillSieve effectively bridges the gap in detecting security issues in multimodal payloads, with robust performance and scalability shown in extensive real-world evaluations.

Abstract: OpenClaw's ClawHub marketplace hosts over 13,000 community-contributed agent skills, and between 13% and 26% of them contain security vulnerabilities according to recent audits. Regex scanners miss obfuscated payloads; formal static analyzers cannot read the natural language instructions in SKILL.md files where prompt injection and social engineering attacks hide. Neither approach handles both modalities. SkillSieve is a three-layer detection framework that applies progressively deeper analysis only where needed. Layer 1 runs regex, AST, and metadata checks through an XGBoost-based feature scorer, filtering roughly 86% of benign skills in under 40ms on average at zero API cost. Layer 2 sends suspicious skills to an LLM, but instead of asking one broad question, it splits the analysis into four parallel sub-tasks (intent alignment, permission justification, covert behavior detection, cross-file consistency), each with its own prompt and structured output. Layer 3 puts high-risk skills before a jury of three different LLMs that vote independently and, if they disagree, debate before reaching a verdict. We evaluate on 49,592 real ClawHub skills and adversarial samples across five evasion techniques, running the full pipeline on a 440 ARM single-board computer. On a 400-skill labeled benchmark, SkillSieve achieves 0.800 F1, outperforming ClawVet's 0.421, at an average cost of 0.006 per skill. Code, data, and benchmark are open-sourced.

</details>


### [454] [FedDetox: Robust Federated SLM Alignment via On-Device Data Sanitization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06833)
*Shunan Zhu,Jiawei Chen,Yonghao Yu,Hideya Ochiai*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces 'FedDetox,' an approach to counter unintended data poisoning in Federated Learning by using knowledge distillation to align safety in small language models on edge devices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the problem of unintended data poisoning in Federated Learning, where real-world client data may include unsafe or toxic information, harming model safety alignment.

Method: The FedDetox framework uses knowledge distillation from large, safety-aligned teacher models to lightweight student classifiers. Edge devices detect unsafe data and replace it with refusal templates during federated learning.

Result: FedDetox maintains the safety alignment of models akin to centralized baselines without sacrificing general model utility.

Conclusion: The framework successfully achieves a safer federated learning process by preemptively addressing unsafe data at the source, demonstrating its potential for real-world implementation.

Abstract: As high quality public data becomes scarce, Federated Learning (FL) provides a vital pathway to leverage valuable private user data while preserving privacy. However, real-world client data often contains toxic or unsafe information. This leads to a critical issue we define as unintended data poisoning, which can severely damage the safety alignment of global models during federated alignment. To address this, we propose FedDetox, a robust framework tailored for Small Language Models (SLMs) on resource-constrained edge devices. We first employ knowledge distillation to transfer sophisticated safety alignment capabilities from large scale safety aligned teacher models into light weight student classifiers suitable for resource constrained edge devices. Specifically, during federated learning for human preference alignment, the edge client identifies unsafe samples at the source and replaces them with refusal templates, effectively transforming potential poisons into positive safety signals. Experiments demonstrate that our approach preserves model safety at a level comparable to centralized baselines without compromising general utility.

</details>


### [455] [RPM-Net Reciprocal Point MLP Network for Unknown Network Security Threat Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06638)
*Jiachen Zhang,Yueming Lu,Fan Feng,Zhanfeng Wang,Shengli Pan,Daoqi Han*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper proposes RPM-Net, a novel framework to detect unknown network threats, especially in imbalanced data scenarios. It overcomes limitations in interpretability and performance seen in existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address challenges in detecting unknown network security threats caused by class imbalance and lack of interpretability in existing techniques.

Method: The authors introduce RPM-Net, a framework utilizing a reciprocal point mechanism for "non-class" representation learning and geometric interpretability, alongside Fisher discriminant regularization (RPM-Net++).

Result: RPM-Net demonstrates superior detection performance across multiple metrics, outperforming existing methods in experimental tests.

Conclusion: RPM-Net provides a significant improvement in detecting unknown threats, with enhanced interpretability and practical applicability for network security.

Abstract: Effective detection of unknown network security threats in multi-class imbalanced environments is critical for maintaining cyberspace security. Current methods focus on learning class representations but face challenges with unknown threat detection, class imbalance, and lack of interpretability, limiting their practical use. To address this, we propose RPM-Net, a novel framework that introduces reciprocal point mechanism to learn "non-class" representations for each known attack category, coupled with adversarial margin constraints that provide geometric interpretability for unknown threat detection. RPM-Net++ further enhances performance through Fisher discriminant regularization. Experimental results show that RPM-Net achieves superior performance across multiple metrics including F1-score, AUROC, and AUPR-OUT, significantly outperforming existing methods and offering practical value for real-world network security applications. Our code is available at:https://github.com/chiachen-chang/RPM-Net

</details>


### [456] [DDP-SA: Scalable Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning via Distributed Differential Privacy and Secure Aggregation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07125)
*Wenjing Wei,Farid Nait-Abdesselam,Alla Jammine*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces DDP-SA, a scalable framework combining local differential privacy (LDP) and additive secret sharing (ASS) to enhance privacy-preserving federated learning by ensuring both stronger privacy and computational feasibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for privacy in federated learning primarily rely on either differential privacy (DP) or secure multi-party computation (MPC), each having limitations. The motivation is to address these gaps by combining the strengths of both approaches for a more secure and practical solution.

Method: The proposed framework employs a two-stage mechanism: local gradients are first perturbed with Laplace noise for LDP, and then the noisy gradients are split into additive secret shares distributed across intermediate servers, ensuring robustness against breaches and protecting individual contributions.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that DDP-SA achieves notably higher model accuracy compared to standalone LDP and delivers stronger privacy protection than methods relying solely on MPC, scaling well with an increasing number of participants.

Conclusion: DDP-SA offers a scalable, privacy-preserving framework for federated learning by effectively combining LDP and ASS, providing enhanced security and feasible computation for real-world applications.

Abstract: This article presents DDP-SA, a scalable privacy-preserving federated learning framework that jointly leverages client-side local differential privacy (LDP) and full-threshold additive secret sharing (ASS) for secure aggregation. Unlike existing methods that rely solely on differential privacy or on secure multi-party computation (MPC), DDP-SA integrates both techniques to deliver stronger end-to-end privacy guarantees while remaining computationally practical. The framework introduces a two-stage protection mechanism: clients first perturb their local gradients with calibrated Laplace noise, then decompose the noisy gradients into additive secret shares that are distributed across multiple intermediate servers. This design ensures that (i) no single compromised server or communication channel can reveal any information about individual client updates, and (ii) the parameter server reconstructs only the aggregated noisy gradient, never any client-specific contribution. Extensive experiments show that DDP-SA achieves substantially higher model accuracy than standalone LDP while providing stronger privacy protection than MPC-only approaches. The proposed framework scales linearly with the number of participants and offers a practical, privacy-preserving solution for federated learning applications with controllable computational and communication overhead.

</details>


### [457] [SkillTrojan: Backdoor Attacks on Skill-Based Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06811)
*Yunhao Feng,Yifan Ding,Yingshui Tan,Boren Zheng,Yanming Guo,Xiaolong Li,Kun Zhai,Yishan Li,Wenke Huang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces SkillTrojan, a backdoor attack targeting skill-based agent systems by embedding malicious code into reusable skills.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Skill-based systems are modular and scalable but introduce risks through unexamined attack surfaces, motivating the need to evaluate their security weaknesses.

Method: SkillTrojan hides malicious logic within skills, partitions encrypted payloads, and activates them via predefined triggers. Skills are auto-generated to spread threats across systems.

Result: SkillTrojan achieves high attack success rates (up to 97.2%) with minimal impact on clean behavior (89.3% accuracy). A dataset of 3,000+ backdoored skills was created for evaluation.

Conclusion: Skill-based agent systems are vulnerable to skill-level backdoors that exploit composition mechanisms, signaling a need for defenses considering skill security.

Abstract: Skill-based agent systems tackle complex tasks by composing reusable skills, improving modularity and scalability while introducing a largely unexamined security attack surface. We propose SkillTrojan, a backdoor attack that targets skill implementations rather than model parameters or training data. SkillTrojan embeds malicious logic inside otherwise plausible skills and leverages standard skill composition to reconstruct and execute an attacker-specified payload. The attack partitions an encrypted payload across multiple benign-looking skill invocations and activates only under a predefined trigger. SkillTrojan also supports automated synthesis of backdoored skills from arbitrary skill templates, enabling scalable propagation across skill-based agent ecosystems. To enable systematic evaluation, we release a dataset of 3,000+ curated backdoored skills spanning diverse skill patterns and trigger-payload configurations. We instantiate SkillTrojan in a representative code-based agent setting and evaluate both clean-task utility and attack success rate. Our results show that skill-level backdoors can be highly effective with minimal degradation of benign behavior, exposing a critical blind spot in current skill-based agent architectures and motivating defenses that explicitly reason about skill composition and execution. Concretely, on EHR SQL, SkillTrojan attains up to 97.2% ASR while maintaining 89.3% clean ACC on GPT-5.2-1211-Global.

</details>


### [458] [Towards Privacy-Preserving Large Language Model: Text-free Inference Through Alignment and Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06831)
*Jeongho Yoon,Chanhee Park,Yongchan Chun,Hyeonseok Moon,Heuiseok Lim*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces Privacy-Preserving Fine-Tuning (PPFT), a method to enhance privacy in LLM-based services by transmitting prompt embeddings instead of raw text, ensuring minimal performance degradation while mitigating privacy risks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address privacy risks posed by transmitting sensitive raw text in current LLM-based systems, which may expose personal, medical, or legal information.

Method: PPFT involves training a client-side encoder with a server-side projection module and LLM to use k-pooled prompt embeddings instead of raw text, followed by fine-tuning these components on domain-specific data using noise-injected embeddings.

Result: PPFT achieves a balance between privacy and utility, showing competitive model performance with minimal degradation compared to non-noisy benchmarks in both domain-specific and general experiments.

Conclusion: Privacy risks in LLM services can be mitigated effectively using PPFT, ensuring data security without sacrificing model performance.

Abstract: Current LLM-based services typically require users to submit raw text regardless of its sensitivity. While intuitive, such practice introduces substantial privacy risks, as unauthorized access may expose personal, medical, or legal information. Although prior defenses strived to mitigate these risks, they often incur substantial computational overhead and degrade model performance. To overcome this privacy-efficiency trade-off, we introduce Privacy-Preserving Fine-Tuning (PPFT), a novel training pipeline that eliminates the need for transmitting raw prompt text while maintaining a favorable balance between privacy preservation and model utility for both clients and service providers. Our approach operates in two stages: first, we train a client-side encoder together with a server-side projection module and LLM, enabling the server to condition on k-pooled prompt embeddings instead of raw text; second, we fine-tune the projection module and LLM on private, domain-specific data using noise-injected embeddings, allowing effective adaptation without exposing plain text prompts and requiring access to the decoder's internal parameters. Extensive experiments on domain-specific and general benchmarks demonstrate that PPFT achieves a striking balance between privacy and utility, maintaining competitive performance with minimal degradation compared to noise-free upper bounds.

</details>


### [459] [Validated Intent Compilation for Constrained Routing in LEO Mega-Constellations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07264)
*Yuanhang Li*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper presents a system for translating high-level operator intents into network routing configurations for LEO mega-constellations, achieving high safety, accuracy, and efficiency through advanced AI techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to simplify and automate the complex task of converting natural language operator intents into low-level network routing constraints for operating LEO mega-constellations, which presently requires both domain expertise and computational effort.

Method: The authors designed a system with three components: (1) a fast and efficient GNN router, (2) a language model-based intent compiler, and (3) a validator ensuring safety and feasibility of intent compilations.

Result: Results showed high performance across all components: 99.8% packet delivery, 98.4% compilation rate, and 0% unsafe acceptance of infeasible intents. End-to-end system evaluation validated correctness and safety across scenarios, outperforming baseline methods significantly.

Conclusion: The proposed system demonstrates its ability to bridge the gap between human operator intents and safe, optimal network routing configurations, providing a robust solution for deployment in complex operational environments like LEO mega-constellations.

Abstract: Operating LEO mega-constellations requires translating high-level operator intents ("reroute financial traffic away from polar links under 80 ms") into low-level routing constraints -- a task that demands both natural language understanding and network-domain expertise. We present an end-to-end system comprising three components: (1) a GNN cost-to-go router that distills Dijkstra-quality routing into a 152K-parameter graph attention network achieving 99.8% packet delivery ratio with 17x inference speedup; (2) an LLM intent compiler that converts natural language to a typed constraint intermediate representation using few-shot prompting with a verifier-feedback repair loop, achieving 98.4% compilation rate and 87.6% full semantic match on feasible intents in a 240-intent benchmark (193 feasible, 47 infeasible); and (3) an 8-pass deterministic validator with constructive feasibility certification that achieves 0% unsafe acceptance on all 47 infeasible intents (30 labeled + 17 discovered by Pass 8), with 100% corruption detection across 240 structural corruption tests and 100% on 15 targeted adversarial attacks. End-to-end evaluation across four constrained routing scenarios confirms zero constraint violations with both routers. We further demonstrate that apparent performance gaps in polar-avoidance scenarios are largely explained by topological reachability ceilings rather than routing quality, and that the LLM compiler outperforms a rule-based baseline by 46.2 percentage points on compositional intents. Our system bridges the semantic gap between operator intent and network configuration while maintaining the safety guarantees required for operational deployment.

</details>


<div id='physics.comp-ph'></div>

# physics.comp-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [460] [Operator Learning for Surrogate Modeling of Wave-Induced Forces from Sea Surface Waves](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06433)
*Shukai Cai,Sourav Dutta,Mark Loveland,Eirik Valseth,Peter Rivera-Casillas,Corey Trahan,Clint Dawson*

Main category: physics.comp-ph

TL;DR: The paper explores the use of Deep Operator Networks (DeepONets) as a computationally efficient surrogate for traditional numerical wave models, specifically SWAN, in storm surge prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Complex and computationally expensive traditional numerical wave models, like SWAN, hinder high-resolution wave and circulation coupling for accurate storm surge prediction. 

Method: The study proposes and tests DeepONets as a surrogate wave model, assessing performance through three different 1-D and 2-D steady-state numerical scenarios with varying boundary wave conditions and wind fields.

Result: DeepONets demonstrated strong accuracy in predicting radiation stress gradients and significant wave heights, even in realistic simulations like Duck, NC.

Conclusion: DeepONets can serve as a computationally efficient and accurate surrogate for numerical wave models, offering improved temporal resolution in practical wave-circulation coupled simulations.

Abstract: Wave setup plays a significant role in transferring wave-induced energy to currents and causing an increase in water elevation. This excess momentum flux, known as radiation stress, motivates the coupling of circulation models with wave models to improve the accuracy of storm surge prediction, however, traditional numerical wave models are complex and computationally expensive. As a result, in practical coupled simulations, wave models are often executed at much coarser temporal resolution than circulation models. In this work, we explore the use of Deep Operator Networks (DeepONets) as a surrogate for the Simulating WAves Nearshore (SWAN) numerical wave model. The proposed surrogate model was tested on three distinct 1-D and 2-D steady-state numerical examples with variable boundary wave conditions and wind fields. When applied to a realistic numerical example of steady state wave simulation in Duck, NC, the model achieved consistently high accuracy in predicting the components of the radiation stress gradient and the significant wave height across representative scenarios.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [461] [Interference Suppression for Massive MU-MIMO Long-Term Beamforming with Matrix Inversion Approximation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06355)
*Amirreza Kiani,Ali Rasteh,Marco Mezzavilla,Sundeep Rangan*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper introduces a subspace nulling approach to improve numerical stability in LTBF for massive MIMO systems by mitigating dominant interferers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dominant interferers degrade the numerical stability of LTBF covariance matrix inversions in massive MIMO systems, affecting performance.

Method: A subspace nulling approach where the signal is projected onto the orthogonal complement of dominant interference subspace to precondition LTBF.

Result: Through ray-tracing in 5G scenarios, the method reduced the number of CG iterations and improved stability while maintaining low overhead.

Conclusion: The proposed method enhances LTBF performance in finite-precision implementations and is computationally efficient for massive MIMO systems.

Abstract: Long-term beamforming (LTBF) is a widely-used scalable alternative to instantaneous multi-user MIMO processing that leverages slowly varying spatial channel statistics. VLSI implementations require matrix inversion that become computationally challenging for massive MIMO systems with large number of antennas. In this work, we show that dominant interferers significantly degrade the numerical conditioning of the LTBF covariance matrix, leading to severe performance loss in finite-precision implementations of polynomial and conjugate gradient (CG) based inversion methods. To address this issue, we propose a subspace nulling approach that operates solely on long-term channel statistics and acts as an implicit preconditioning step for LTBF. By projecting the received signal onto the orthogonal complement of the dominant interference subspace, the proposed method reduces the eigenvalue spread of the covariance matrix and improves numerical stability. Through ray-tracing simulations in a realistic 5G scenario, we demonstrate that the proposed method substantially reduces the number of CG iterations required to achieve near-optimal performance across floating-point and fixed-point implementations while preserving the low-overhead nature of LTBF.

</details>


### [462] [Development of ML model for triboelectric nanogenerator based sign language detection system](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06220)
*Meshv Patel,Bikash Baro,Sayan Bayan,Mohendra Roy*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper presents a study comparing machine learning (ML) and deep learning models for gesture recognition using a TENG-based sensor glove. The proposed MFCC CNN-LSTM architecture achieves superior performance compared to traditional ML and other time-domain deep learning methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of vision-based sign language recognition systems, including occlusion and computational costs, and explore effective alternatives using wearable sensor-based solutions for bridging communication gaps between deaf and hearing communities.

Method: The study uses a TENG-based sensor glove with five flex sensors to collect multivariate time-series data for 11 sign classes. It evaluates ML, feedforward neural networks, LSTM-based models, and a frequency-domain MFCC CNN-LSTM architecture. Ablation studies and data augmentation (time warping, noise injection) are employed to fine-tune temporal context and generalization.

Result: The proposed MFCC CNN-LSTM architecture achieves 93.33% accuracy and 95.56% precision, outperforming the best ML algorithm (Random Forest: 70.38%) by 23 points. Optimal 50-timestep windows provide a balance between context and data volume, with MFCC feature extraction and data augmentation significantly improving results.

Conclusion: Frequency-domain feature representations and multi-sensor processing architectures, like MFCC CNN-LSTM, provide substantial performance gains over classical ML algorithms and time-domain deep learning approaches, offering a promising path for wearable gesture recognition in assistive technologies.

Abstract: Sign language recognition (SLR) is vital for bridging communication gaps between deaf and hearing communities. Vision-based approaches suffer from occlusion, computational costs, and physical constraints. This work presents a comparison of machine learning (ML) and deep learning models for a custom triboelectric nanogenerator (TENG)-based sensor glove. Utilizing multivariate time-series data from five flex sensors, the study benchmarks traditional ML algorithms, feedforward neural networks, LSTM-based temporal models, and a multi-sensor MFCC CNN-LSTM architecture across 11 sign classes (digits 1-5, letters A-F). The proposed MFCC CNN-LSTM architecture processes frequency-domain features from each sensor through independent convolutional branches before fusion. It achieves 93.33% accuracy and 95.56% precision, a 23-point improvement over the best ML algorithm (Random Forest: 70.38%). Ablation studies reveal 50-timestep windows offer a tradeoff between temporal context and training data volume, yielding 84.13% accuracy compared to 58.06% with 100-timestep windows. MFCC feature extraction maps temporal variations to execution-speed-invariant spectral representations, and data augmentation methods (time warping, noise injection) are essential for generalization. Results demonstrate that frequency-domain feature representations combined with parallel multi-sensor processing architectures offer enhancement over classical algorithms and time-domain deep learning for wearable sensor-based gesture recognition. This aids assistive technology development.

</details>


### [463] [ELC: Evidential Lifelong Classifier for Uncertainty Aware Radar Pulse Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06958)
*Mohamed Rabie,Chinthana Panagamuwa,Konstantinos G. Kyriakopoulos*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The study introduces Evidential Lifelong Classifier (ELC), which integrates uncertainty modeling and lifelong learning for radar pulse classification. ELC outperforms a Bayesian approach in uncertain, low-SNR conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Radar pulse classification in Electromagnetic Warfare requires reliable learning of new pulses and mechanisms for predictive confidence to aid in decision-making.

Method: The authors combine Uncertainty Quantification and Lifelong Learning, introducing the ELC model based on evidence theory. ELC is benchmarked against Bayesian Lifelong Classifier (BLC) using Learn-Prune-Share and uncertainty-based selective prediction.

Result: ELC achieves up to 46% better recall than BLC in synthetic radar pulse datasets at -20 dB SNR through effective uncertainty assessment and selective prediction.

Conclusion: Evidential uncertainty contributes significantly to model trustworthiness by enhancing its ability to express ignorance and address unreliable predictions in challenging scenarios.

Abstract: Reliable radar pulse classification is essential in Electromagnetic Warfare for situational awareness and decision support. Deep Neural Networks have shown strong performance in radar pulse and RF emitter recognition; however, on their own they struggle to efficiently learn new pulses and lack mechanisms for expressing predictive confidence. This paper integrates Uncertainty Quantification with Lifelong Learning to address both challenges. The proposed approach is an Evidential Lifelong Classifier (ELC), which models epistemic uncertainty using evidence theory. ELC is evaluated against a Bayesian Lifelong Classifier (BLC), which quantifies uncertainty through Shannon entropy. Both integrate Learn-Prune-Share to enable continual learning of new pulses and uncertainty-based selective prediction to reject unreliable predictions. ELC and BLC are evaluated on 2 synthetic radar and 3 RF fingerprinting datasets. Selective prediction based on evidential uncertainty improves recall by up to 46% at -20 dB SNR on synthetic radar pulse datasets, highlighting its effectiveness at identifying unreliable predictions in low-SNR conditions compared to BLC. These findings demonstrate that evidential uncertainty offers a strong correlation between confidence and correctness, improving the trustworthiness of ELC by allowing it to express ignorance.

</details>


<div id='quant-ph'></div>

# quant-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [464] [Soft-Quantum Algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06523)
*Basil Kyriacou,Mo Kordzanganeh,Maniraman Periyasamy,Alexey Melnikov*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: This paper introduces a faster method for training variational quantum circuits by directly optimizing unitary matrices with a regularization term and later transforming them into gate-based architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the high training cost and low fidelity of current quantum devices, which limit the practicality of quantum machine learning.

Method: The method involves a two-step process: (1) Directly optimizing matrix elements with unitarity ensured via a regularization term added to the loss function, and (2) Circuit alignment to transform optimized matrices back into gate-based architectures.

Result: The proposed method significantly reduces training time for a five-qubit supervised classification task (from over 2 hours to less than 4 minutes) and achieves better binary cross-entropy loss. It also demonstrated improved performance in a reinforcement learning cartpole task using a hybrid quantum-classical network compared to classical methods.

Conclusion: Direct matrix training with unitarity regularization is faster and effective for variational quantum circuits, enabling efficient and practical implementations in both supervised and reinforcement learning tasks.

Abstract: Quantum operations on pure states can be fully represented by unitary matrices. Variational quantum circuits, also known as quantum neural networks, embed data and trainable parameters into gate-based operations and optimize the parameters via gradient descent. The high cost of training and low fidelity of current quantum devices, however, restricts much of quantum machine learning to classical simulation. For few-qubit problems with large datasets, training the matrix elements directly, as is done with weight matrices in classical neural networks, can be faster than decomposing data and parameters into gates. We propose a method that trains matrices directly while maintaining unitarity through a single regularization term added to the loss function. A second training step, circuit alignment, then recovers a gate-based architecture from the resulting soft-unitary. On a five-qubit supervised classification task with 1000 datapoints, this two-step process produces a trained variational circuit in under four minutes, compared to over two hours for direct circuit training, while achieving lower binary cross-entropy loss. In a second experiment, soft-unitaries are embedded in a hybrid quantum-classical network for a reinforcement learning cartpole task, where the hybrid agent outperforms a purely classical baseline of comparable size.

</details>


### [465] [QNAS: A Neural Architecture Search Framework for Accurate and Efficient Quantum Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07013)
*Kooshan Maleki,Alberto Marchisio,Muhammad Shafique*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: The paper introduces QNAS, a framework for designing quantum neural networks (QNNs), focusing on balancing accuracy, hardware efficiency, and cutting overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges posed by designing deployable and accurate QNNs under the constraints of limited qubits and the need for circuit cutting.

Method: QNAS uses a shared parameter SuperCircuit and NSGA-II optimization to jointly evaluate validation error, runtime cost, and circuit cutting overhead across few epochs of training.

Result: QNAS identifies optimal hybrid quantum-classical neural network (HQNN) designs that achieve high accuracy and efficiency on benchmarks such as MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and Iris datasets.

Conclusion: QNAS offers a unified and automated approach to guide the design of QNNs, revealing optimal architectures that balance performance and practical constraints for NISQ hardware.

Abstract: Designing quantum neural networks (QNNs) that are both accurate and deployable on NISQ hardware is challenging. Handcrafted ansatze must balance expressivity, trainability, and resource use, while limited qubits often necessitate circuit cutting. Existing quantum architecture search methods primarily optimize accuracy while only heuristically controlling quantum and mostly ignore the exponential overhead of circuit cutting. We introduce QNAS, a neural architecture search framework that unifies hardware aware evaluation, multi objective optimization, and cutting overhead awareness for hybrid quantum classical neural networks (HQNNs). QNAS trains a shared parameter SuperCircuit and uses NSGA-II to optimize three objectives jointly: (i) validation error, (ii) a runtime cost proxy measuring wall clock evaluation time, and (iii) the estimated number of subcircuits under a target qubit budget. QNAS evaluates candidate HQNNs under a few epochs of training and discovers clear Pareto fronts that reveal tradeoffs between accuracy, efficiency, and cutting overhead. Across MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and Iris benchmarks, we observe that embedding type and CNOT mode selection significantly impact both accuracy and efficiency, with angle-y embedding and sparse entangling patterns outperforming other configurations on image datasets, and amplitude embedding excelling on tabular data (Iris). On MNIST, the best architecture achieves 97.16% test accuracy with a compact 8 qubit, 2 layer circuit; on the more challenging Fashion-MNIST, 87.38% with a 5 qubit, 2 layer circuit; and on Iris, 100% validation accuracy with a 4 qubit, 2 layer circuit. QNAS surfaces these design insights automatically during search, guiding practitioners toward architectures that balance accuracy, resource efficiency, and practical deployability on current hardware.

</details>


<div id='cs.MA'></div>

# cs.MA [[Back]](#toc)

### [466] [Logical Robots: Declarative Multi-Agent Programming in Logica](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06629)
*Evgeny Skvortsov,Yilin Xia,Ojaswa Garg,Shawn Bowers,Bertram Ludäscher*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper introduces Logical Robots, a platform for programming robot behaviors using a logic-based language called Logica.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create an integrated framework where low-level reactive control and high-level planning can coexist for multi-agent robot simulations.

Method: Robot behaviors are declaratively defined using logical predicates that process sensory observations to determine motor outputs in the Logica programming language.

Result: The platform simplifies the exploration and specification of multi-agent robotics using a unified logic-programming paradigm.

Conclusion: Logical Robots enable a cohesive environment for specifying and studying autonomous behaviors in robots using logical programming approaches.

Abstract: We present Logical Robots, an interactive multi-agent simulation platform where autonomous robot behavior is specified declaratively in the logic programming language Logica. Robot behavior is defined by logical predicates that map observations from simulated radar arrays and shared memory to desired motor outputs. This approach allows low-level reactive control and high-level planning to coexist within a single programming environment, providing a coherent framework for exploring multi-agent robot behavior.

</details>


### [467] [Strategic Persuasion with Trait-Conditioned Multi-Agent Systems for Iterative Legal Argumentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07028)
*Philipp D. Siedler*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper introduces a simulation framework for legal argumentation using Large Language Model (LLM) agents with controlled traits. Findings show heterogeneous teams excel and adaptive strategies enhance performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore how language and rhetoric influence strategic outcomes in domains like law and diplomacy, which are traditionally abstracted in game-theoretic models.

Method: The researchers develop the Strategic Courtroom Framework, enabling prosecution and defense LLM agents with predefined traits to simulate legal discourse in iterative trials. 7,000 simulations across various configurations were analyzed.

Result: Heterogeneous teams performed better than homogeneous ones, moderate trial interaction stabilized outcomes, and traits like quantitative and charismatic were pivotal for persuasion. Adaptive dynamic strategies outperformed static approaches.

Conclusion: Language can serve as a strategic tool in multi-agent settings, and intelligently designed systems can leverage rhetoric and adaptability for success in adversarial interactions.

Abstract: Strategic interaction in adversarial domains such as law, diplomacy, and negotiation is mediated by language, yet most game-theoretic models abstract away the mechanisms of persuasion that operate through discourse. We present the Strategic Courtroom Framework, a multi-agent simulation environment in which prosecution and defense teams composed of trait-conditioned Large Language Model (LLM) agents engage in iterative, round-based legal argumentation. Agents are instantiated using nine interpretable traits organized into four archetypes, enabling systematic control over rhetorical style and strategic orientation.
  We evaluate the framework across 10 synthetic legal cases and 84 three-trait team configurations, totaling over 7{,}000 simulated trials using DeepSeek-R1 and Gemini~2.5~Pro. Our results show that heterogeneous teams with complementary traits consistently outperform homogeneous configurations, that moderate interaction depth yields more stable verdicts, and that certain traits (notably quantitative and charismatic) contribute disproportionately to persuasive success. We further introduce a reinforcement-learning-based Trait Orchestrator that dynamically generates defense traits conditioned on the case and opposing team, discovering strategies that outperform static, human-designed trait combinations.
  Together, these findings demonstrate how language can be treated as a first-class strategic action space and provide a foundation for building autonomous agents capable of adaptive persuasion in multi-agent environments.

</details>


### [468] [AgentCity: Constitutional Governance for Autonomous Agent Economies via Separation of Power](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.07007)
*Anbang Ruan,Xing Zhang*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Separation of Power (SoP) model for governing autonomous AI agents working across organizational boundaries. It proposes a blockchain-based structure to ensure accountability and alignment with human intent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the governance challenges posed by autonomous AI agents collaborating at scale without centralized oversight, leading to emergent, opaque behaviors.

Method: The authors introduce the SoP model, which separates legislative (smart contracts), execution (deterministic software), and adjudicative (human accountability) functions. They implement this model in a blockchain-based system called AgentCity.

Result: The study includes a pre-registered experiment with 50-1,000 AI agents in a commons production economy. The experiment aims to validate the thesis of alignment-through-accountability.

Conclusion: The proposed SoP model ensures decentralized governance by aligning AI agents with their human owners through an accountability chain, fostering collective behavior aligned with human values without centralized control.

Abstract: Autonomous AI agents are beginning to operate across organizational boundaries on the open internet -- discovering, transacting with, and delegating to agents owned by other parties without centralized oversight. When agents from different human principals collaborate at scale, the collective becomes opaque: no single human can observe, audit, or govern the emergent behavior. We term this the Logic Monopoly -- the agent society's unchecked monopoly over the entire logic chain from planning through execution to evaluation. We propose the Separation of Power (SoP) model, a constitutional governance architecture deployed on public blockchain that breaks this monopoly through three structural separations: agents legislate operational rules as smart contracts, deterministic software executes within those contracts, and humans adjudicate through a complete ownership chain binding every agent to a responsible principal. In this architecture, smart contracts are the law itself -- the actual legislative output that agents produce and that governs their behavior. We instantiate SoP in AgentCity on an EVM-compatible layer-2 blockchain (L2) with a three-tier contract hierarchy (foundational, meta, and operational). The core thesis is alignment-through-accountability: if each agent is aligned with its human owner through the accountability chain, then the collective converges on behavior aligned with human intent -- without top-down rules. A pre-registered experiment evaluates this thesis in a commons production economy -- where agents share a finite resource pool and collaboratively produce value -- at 50-1,000 agent scale.

</details>


<div id='cs.GT'></div>

# cs.GT [[Back]](#toc)

### [469] [Incentive-Aware Multi-Fidelity Optimization for Generative Advertising in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06263)
*Jiayuan Liu,Barry Wang,Jiarui Gan,Tonghan Wang,Leon Xie,Mingyu Guo,Vincent Conitzer*

Main category: cs.GT

TL;DR: The paper introduces IAMFM, a framework for optimizing advertising sponsorships in LLM responses while addressing constraints like advertiser behavior and computation costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The research aims to solve the problem of efficiently incorporating sponsorships into generative responses by addressing advertiser strategies and the high computational cost of stochastic generation.

Method: The method involves the introduction of the IAMFM framework, which combines VCG incentives with Multi-Fidelity Optimization. It includes two algorithmic approaches and introduces the Active Counterfactual Optimization for payment efficiency.

Result: IAMFM provides guarantees for strategy-proofness and individual rationality and shows superior performance over single-fidelity baselines across different budget scenarios.

Conclusion: The research presents a generalizable, efficient method for aligning incentives and managing budgets in generative advertising processes for LLMs.

Abstract: Generative advertising in large language model (LLM) responses requires optimizing sponsorship configurations under two strict constraints: the strategic behavior of advertisers and the high cost of stochastic generations. To address this, we propose the Incentive-Aware Multi-Fidelity Mechanism (IAMFM), a unified framework coupling Vickrey-Clarke-Groves (VCG) incentives with Multi-Fidelity Optimization to maximize expected social welfare. We compare two algorithmic instantiations (elimination-based and model-based), revealing their budget-dependent performance trade-offs. Crucially, to make VCG computationally feasible, we introduce Active Counterfactual Optimization, a "warm-start" approach that reuses optimization data for efficient payment calculation. We provide formal guarantees for approximate strategy-proofness and individual rationality, establishing a general approach for incentive-aligned, budget-constrained generative processes. Experiments demonstrate that IAMFM outperforms single-fidelity baselines across diverse budgets.

</details>


### [470] [Revisiting Fairness Impossibility with Endogenous Behavior](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06378)
*Elizabeth Maggie Penn,John W. Patty*

Main category: cs.GT

TL;DR: This paper explores the consequences of algorithmic classification when people strategically respond to it, proposing a design that balances fairness across groups by adjusting consequences but introduces new trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate how behavioral responses to algorithmic classifications, driven by the changing consequences (stakes), impact fairness and classic impossibility results in algorithmic fairness.

Method: The authors develop a two-stage design: first ensuring standardized statistical performance across groups; second, adjusting stakes to induce comparable behavioral patterns, even if it means treating groups differently in identical classification decisions.

Result: The analysis finds that incompatibility between error-rate balance and predictive parity disappears, but introduces new forms of unequal treatment that depend on adjusting stakes for fairness.

Conclusion: Fairness in algorithmic systems must consider how classification interacts with behavioral responses and consequences, treating human outcomes as primary design variables, which raises new trade-offs and normative challenges.

Abstract: In many real-world settings, institutions can and do adjust the consequences attached to algorithmic classification decisions, such as the size of fines, sentence lengths, or benefit levels. We refer to these consequences as the stakes associated with classification. These stakes can give rise to behavioral responses to classification, as people adjust their actions in anticipation of how they will be classified. Much of the algorithmic fairness literature evaluates classification outcomes while holding behavior fixed, treating behavioral differences across groups as exogenous features of the environment. Under this assumption, the stakes of classification play no role in shaping outcomes.
  We revisit classic impossibility results in algorithmic fairness in a setting where people respond strategically to classification. We show that, in this environment, the well-known incompatibility between error-rate balance and predictive parity disappears, but only by potentially introducing a qualitatively different form of unequal treatment. Concretely, we construct a two-stage design in which a classifier first standardizes its statistical performance across groups, and then adjusts stakes so as to induce comparable patterns of behavior. This requires treating groups differently in the consequences attached to identical classification decisions. Our results demonstrate that fairness in strategic settings cannot be assessed solely by how algorithms map data into decisions. Rather, our analysis treats the human consequences of classification as primary design variables, introduces normative criteria governing their use, and shows that their interaction with statistical fairness criteria generates qualitatively new tradeoffs. Our aim is to make these tradeoffs precise and explicit.

</details>


### [471] [Beyond Pessimism: Offline Learning in KL-regularized Games](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06738)
*Yuheng Zhang,Claire Chen,Nan Jiang*

Main category: cs.GT

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel algorithm for KL-regularized two-player zero-sum games, achieving improved statistical rates of $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(1/n)$ without relying on pessimism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches in KL-regularized games rely on pessimistic value estimation, which limits statistical rates to $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(1/\sqrt n)$. A better framework is needed to address these limitations and improve sample efficiency.

Method: The authors leverage the smoothness property of KL-regularized best responses and the stability of Nash equilibrium induced by skew symmetry. They propose a new algorithm that replaces the reliance on pessimism for addressing distribution shift.

Result: The new algorithm achieves a $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(1/n)$ statistical rate for offline learning in KL-regularized zero-sum games, marking a significant improvement over previous approaches. Additionally, an efficient self-play policy optimization algorithm is proposed that maintains this improved rate.

Conclusion: The study establishes a more efficient approach to KL-regularized zero-sum games, advancing both theoretical understanding and practical optimization methods by eliminating the need for pessimism while achieving superior sample complexity.

Abstract: We study offline learning in KL-regularized two-player zero-sum games, where policies are optimized under a KL constraint to a fixed reference policy. Prior work relies on pessimistic value estimation to handle distribution shift, yielding only $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(1/\sqrt n)$ statistical rates. We develop a new pessimism-free algorithm and analytical framework for KL-regularized games, built on the smoothness of KL-regularized best responses and a stability property of the Nash equilibrium induced by skew symmetry. This yields the first $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(1/n)$ sample complexity bound for offline learning in KL-regularized zero-sum games, achieved entirely without pessimism. We further propose an efficient self-play policy optimization algorithm and prove that, with a number of iterations linear in the sample size, it achieves the same fast $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(1/n)$ statistical rate as the minimax estimator.

</details>


<div id='math.NA'></div>

# math.NA [[Back]](#toc)

### [472] [Neural parametric representations for thin-shell shape optimisation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06612)
*Xiao Xiao,Fehmi Cirak*

Main category: math.NA

TL;DR: This paper introduces a neural network-based parameterization (NRep) for optimizing thin-shell structures, aiming for improved flexibility and differentiability in geometry representation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Shape optimization in thin-shell structures needs a flexible and differentiable geometric representation. Existing methods may lack these qualities or struggle with complex geometries.

Method: The authors use a neural network (MLP with periodic activations) for mid-surface parameterization. Gradient-based structural compliance optimization is undertaken using neural network parameters as design variables.

Result: Benchmark problems show successful optimization and demonstrate effectiveness for classical solutions. NRep appears capable of handling complex lattice-skin structures.

Conclusion: The proposed NRep provides a compact, expressive, and effective method for optimizing thin-shell designs, paving the way for applications to more intricate geometries.

Abstract: Shape optimisation of thin-shell structures requires a flexible, differentiable geometric representation suitable for gradient-based optimisation. We propose a neural parametric representation (NRep) for the shell mid-surface based on a neural network with periodic activation functions. The NRep is defined using a multi-layer perceptron (MLP), which maps the parametric coordinates of mid-surface vertices to their physical coordinates. A structural compliance optimisation problem is posed to optimise the shape of a thin-shell parameterised by the NRep subject to a volume constraint, with the network parameters as design variables. The resulting shape optimisation problem is solved using a gradient-based optimisation algorithm. Benchmark examples with classical solutions demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed NRep. The approach exhibits potential for complex lattice-skin structures, owing to the compact and expressive geometry representation afforded by the NRep.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.QM'></div>

# q-bio.QM [[Back]](#toc)

### [473] [From Exposure to Internalization: Dual-Stream Calibration for In-context Clinical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06262)
*Chuang Zhao,Hongke Zhao,Xiaofang Zhou,Xiaomeng Li*

Main category: q-bio.QM

TL;DR: This paper introduces Dual-Stream Calibration (DSC), a test-time framework enhancing reasoning in clinical contexts by dynamically internalizing data through semantic and structural calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing models like fine-tuning, ICL, and RAG do not effectively internalize contextual nuances during inference, which is critical for accurate clinical reasoning.

Method: The paper proposes a dual-stream approach: the Semantic Calibration Stream for stabilizing semantic evidence and the Structural Calibration Stream for refining inferential dependencies at test time.

Result: The DSC framework outperformed state-of-the-art methods on thirteen clinical datasets and across three task types.

Conclusion: Dual-Stream Calibration offers a paradigm shift from passive knowledge modeling to dynamic reasoning, enabling more coherent and context-sensitive clinical inference.

Abstract: Contextual clinical reasoning demands robust inference grounded in complex, heterogeneous clinical records. While state-of-the-art fine-tuning, in-context learning (ICL), and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) enable knowledge exposure, they often fall short of genuine contextual internalization: dynamically adjusting a model's internal representations to the subtle nuances of individual cases at inference time. To address this, we propose Dual-Stream Calibration (DSC), a test-time training framework that transcends superficial knowledge exposure to achieve deep internalization during inference. DSC facilitates input internalization by synergistically aligning two calibration streams. Unlike passive context exposure, the Semantic Calibration Stream enforces a deliberative reflection on core evidence, internalizing semantic anchors by minimizing entropy to stabilize generative trajectories. Simultaneously, the Structural Calibration Stream assimilates latent inferential dependencies through an iterative meta-learning objective. By training on specialized support sets at test-time, this stream enables the model to bridge the gap between external evidence and internal logic, synthesizing fragmented data into a coherent response. Our approach shifts the reasoning paradigm from passive attention-based matching to an active refinement of the latent inferential space. Validated against thirteen clinical datasets, DSC demonstrates superiority across three distinct task paradigms, consistently outstripping state-of-the-art baselines ranging from training-dependent models to test-time learning frameworks.

</details>


### [474] [ToxReason: A Benchmark for Mechanistic Chemical Toxicity Reasoning via Adverse Outcome Pathway](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06264)
*Jueon Park,Wonjune Jang,Chanhwi Kim,Yein Park,Jaewoo Kang*

Main category: q-bio.QM

TL;DR: The paper introduces ToxReason, a benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) on their ability to reason about toxicity mechanisms and predict toxic outcomes. Results show that reasoning-aware training improves performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current tools for toxicity prediction rely heavily on chemical structure and fail to consider complex biological mechanisms, leading to unreliable predictions. There is a lack of systematic evaluation frameworks for models' mechanistic reasoning.

Method: The authors developed ToxReason, a benchmark rooted in the Adverse Outcome Pathway (AOP) framework, integrating experimental drug-target interactions with toxicity labels. Models are required to reason from molecular initiating events (MIE) to adverse outcomes (AO).

Result: The study found that strong predictive performance among existing LLMs does not imply reliable mechanistic reasoning. Training models with reasoning-aware methods improved both reasoning and toxicity prediction accuracy.

Conclusion: ToxReason emphasizes the need for integrating mechanistic reasoning into both training and evaluation pipelines for more trustworthy and accurate toxicity modeling.

Abstract: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled molecular reasoning for property prediction. However, toxicity arises from complex biological mechanisms beyond chemical structure, necessitating mechanistic reasoning for reliable prediction. Despite its importance, current benchmarks fail to systematically evaluate this capability. LLMs can generate fluent but biologically unfaithful explanations, making it difficult to assess whether predicted toxicities are grounded invalid mechanisms. To bridge this gap, we introduce ToxReason, a benchmark grounded in the Adverse Outcome Pathway (AOP) that evaluates organ-level toxicity reasoning across multiple organs. ToxReason integrates experimental drug-target interaction evidence with toxicity labels, requiring models to infer both toxic outcomes and their underlying mechanisms from Molecular Initiating Event (MIE) to Adverse Outcome (AO). Using ToxReason, we evaluate toxicity prediction performance and reasoning quality across diverse LLMs. We find that strong predictive performance does not necessarily imply reliable reasoning. Furthermore, we show that reasoning-aware training improves mechanistic reasoning and, consequently, toxicity prediction performance. Together, these results underscore the necessity of integrating reasoning into both evaluation and training for trustworthy toxicity modeling.

</details>


### [475] [MAT-Cell: A Multi-Agent Tree-Structured Reasoning Framework for Batch-Level Single-Cell Annotation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06269)
*Yehui Yang,Zelin Zang,Changxi Chi,Jingbo Zhou,Xienan Zheng,Yuzhe Jia,Chang Yu,Jinlin Wu,Fuji Yang,Jiebo Luo,Zhen Lei,Stan Z. Li*

Main category: q-bio.QM

TL;DR: MAT-Cell is a neuro-symbolic reasoning framework for single-cell analysis, addressing generalization and noise challenges in current methods, and significantly outperforming baseline models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated single-cell reasoning struggles with generalization and producing valid biological associations due to reliance on either supervised methods or large language models (LLMs) without proper grounding.

Method: MAT-Cell uses Adaptive Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to integrate biological axioms, mitigating noise, and employs a dialectic verification process to refine reasoning paths by enforcing logical consistency through rebuttal agents.

Result: MAT-Cell achieves superior performance across various benchmarks, including robust results in challenging cross-species scenarios where baseline methods fail.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates that integrating symbolic constraints with neural reasoning enhances accuracy and robustness in single-cell analysis, providing a promising approach to generalization and noise reduction.

Abstract: Automated cellular reasoning faces a core dichotomy: supervised methods fall into the Reference Trap and fail to generalize to out-of-distribution cell states, while large language models (LLMs), without grounded biological priors, suffer from a Signal-to-Noise Paradox that produces spurious associations. We propose MAT-Cell, a neuro-symbolic reasoning framework that reframes single-cell analysis from black-box classification into constructive, verifiable proof generation. MAT-Cell injects symbolic constraints through adaptive Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to ground neural reasoning in biological axioms and reduce transcriptomic noise. It further employs a dialectic verification process with homogeneous rebuttal agents to audit and prune reasoning paths, forming syllogistic derivation trees that enforce logical consistency.Across large-scale and cross-species benchmarks, MAT-Cell significantly outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) models and maintains robust per-formance in challenging scenarios where baselinemethods severely degrade. Code is available at https://gith ub.com/jiangliu91/MAT-Cell-A-Mul ti-Agent-Tree-Structured-Reasoni ng-Framework-for-Batch-Level-Sin gle-Cell-Annotation.

</details>


<div id='physics.plasm-ph'></div>

# physics.plasm-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [476] [Plasma GraphRAG: Physics-Grounded Parameter Selection for Gyrokinetic Simulations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.06279)
